OT4ML
Optimal Transport for Machine Learners
Chapters
Optimal Matching between Point CloudsMonge Problem between MeasuresKantorovich RelaxationDual ProblemSemi-discrete and Wasserstein-1Divergences and Dual NormsEntropic Regularization: Sinkhorn AlgorithmEntropic Regularization: ConvergenceGeneralized Wasserstein DistancesGeneralized OT ProblemsBeyond Comparing MeasuresDynamic Optimal TransportWasserstein Gradient FlowsGenerative Models via Transportation
ConclusionNotation TableIndex

Kantorovich Relaxation

Kantorovich’s relaxation is the decisive move that turns transport into convex optimization. Deterministic maps are replaced by couplings, infeasibility and asymmetry disappear, and the Wasserstein distances emerge. Historically, this linear-programming viewpoint grew from Kantorovich’s economic planning work Kantorovich, 1942 and is now the standard foundation of optimal transport Villani, 2003Villani, 2009Rachev & Rüschendorf, 1998.

from pathlib import Path
import sys

from IPython.display import Image as DisplayImage
from IPython.display import display

here = Path.cwd()
myst_dir = None
for candidate in [here, here.parent, here / "myst", here.parent / "myst", here.parent.parent / "myst"]:
    if (candidate / "ot4ml_web.py").exists():
        myst_dir = candidate.resolve()
        sys.path.insert(0, str(myst_dir))
        break

if myst_dir is None:
    raise RuntimeError("Could not locate myst/ot4ml_web.py")

repo_root = myst_dir.parent
thumbnails = repo_root / "notebooks-figures" / "thumbnails"

def show_book_figure(name, width=760):
    display(DisplayImage(filename=str(thumbnails / f"{name}.png"), width=width))

Discrete Relaxation¶

The discrete relaxation is the cleanest place to see mass splitting. It replaces permutations by a transportation polytope and reveals the linear-programming structure that algorithms exploit.

Monge’s discrete matching problem cannot be applied when the two clouds have different cardinalities or unequal weights. The continuous Monge problem has the same obstruction: there may be no map TTT such that T♯α=βT_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β, for instance when one Dirac mass must be sent to several Dirac masses. It is also asymmetric: two Dirac masses can be mapped to one, but one Dirac mass cannot be split into two by a deterministic map.

Kantorovich’s idea is to relax deterministic transportation. Instead of sending each source point xix_ixi​ to exactly one target, the mass at xix_ixi​ may be dispatched across several targets. The relaxation is encoded by a coupling matrix P∈R+n×mP\in\RR_+^{n\times m}P∈R+n×m​ for two discrete measures

α=∑iaiδxi,β=∑jbjδyj.\al=\sum_i a_i\delta_{x_i}, \qquad \be=\sum_j b_j\delta_{y_j}.α=i∑​ai​δxi​​,β=j∑​bj​δyj​​.
(1)
Definition: Discrete Couplings And Mass Conservation

Admissible couplings are constrained only by conservation of mass:

U(a,b):={P∈R+n×m  :  P1m=a,P⊤1n=b}.\CouplingsD(a,b) \eqdef \left\{ P\in\RR_+^{n\times m} \;:\; P\mathbf{1}_m=a,\quad P^\top\mathbf{1}_n=b \right\}.U(a,b):={P∈R+n×m​:P1m​=a,P⊤1n​=b}.
(2)

Equivalently, rows sum to the source masses and columns sum to the target masses:

(∑jPij)i=a,(∑iPij)j=b.\left(\sum_j P_{ij}\right)_i=a, \qquad \left(\sum_i P_{ij}\right)_j=b.(j∑​Pij​)i​=a,(i∑​Pij​)j​=b.
(3)
Remark: Small transportation polytopes

The definition is already informative in the smallest dimensions. If n=m=1n=m=1n=m=1, mass conservation fixes the only entry, so the feasible set is a singleton. The same happens for (n,m)=(2,1)(n,m)=(2,1)(n,m)=(2,1), and by symmetry for (n,m)=(1,2)(n,m)=(1,2)(n,m)=(1,2): the unique coupling is forced by its only column, or by its only row.

The first nontrivial case is (n,m)=(2,2)(n,m)=(2,2)(n,m)=(2,2). Let a=(p,1−p)\a=(p,1-p)a=(p,1−p) and b=(q,1−q)\b=(q,1-q)b=(q,1−q) with p,q∈[0,1]p,q\in[0,1]p,q∈[0,1]. Once s:=P1,1s\eqdef \P_{1,1}s:=P1,1​ is chosen, the marginal constraints force all other entries, hence every coupling has the form

P(s)=(sp−sq−s1−p−q+s).\P(s)= \begin{pmatrix} s & p-s \\ q-s & 1-p-q+s \end{pmatrix}.P(s)=(sq−s​p−s1−p−q+s​).
(4)

The nonnegativity constraints are exactly

s∈[max⁡(0,p+q−1),min⁡(p,q)],s\in\big[\max(0,p+q-1),\min(p,q)\big],s∈[max(0,p+q−1),min(p,q)],
(5)

so U(a,b)\CouplingsD(\a,\b)U(a,b) is a segment, possibly reduced to a point at the boundary. In general, when all marginal entries are positive, the transportation polytope has affine dimension (n−1)(m−1)(n-1)(m-1)(n−1)(m−1) before the nonnegativity inequalities cut out its faces.

The first consequence is feasibility. There is always at least one admissible plan.

Definition: Discrete Product Coupling

Given weights a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​ and b∈Δmb\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​, the discrete product, or trivial, coupling is

(a⊗b)ij:=aibj.(a\otimes b)_{ij}\eqdef a_i b_j.(a⊗b)ij​:=ai​bj​.
(6)

It belongs to U(a,b)\CouplingsD(a,b)U(a,b) and corresponds to choosing source and target labels independently.

The feasible set is a bounded intersection of an affine space with the nonnegative orthant, hence a convex polytope. In one dimension, the coupling can be read as a matrix: rows index source bins, columns index target bins, and the marginal constraints appear as prescribed row and column sums.

Proposition: Discrete Product Optimality Is Degenerate

Assume that all zero-mass rows and columns have been removed, so that ai>0a_i>0ai​>0 and bj>0b_j>0bj​>0, and let CCC be a finite cost matrix. The product plan a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b minimizes P↦⟨C,P⟩P\mapsto\langle C,P\rangleP↦⟨C,P⟩ over U(a,b)\CouplingsD(a,b)U(a,b) if and only if every coupling P∈U(a,b)P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)P∈U(a,b) minimizes it.

Proof

The reverse implication is immediate. Conversely, assume that a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b is optimal and let Q∈U(a,b)Q\in\CouplingsD(a,b)Q∈U(a,b) be arbitrary. Since all entries of a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b are positive, there exists t>0t>0t>0 small enough that

R:=(1+t)(a⊗b)−tQR\eqdef(1+t)(a\otimes b)-tQR:=(1+t)(a⊗b)−tQ
(7)

is nonnegative. It still has row sums aaa and column sums bbb, so R∈U(a,b)R\in\CouplingsD(a,b)R∈U(a,b). Also

a⊗b=11+tR+t1+tQ.a\otimes b=\frac{1}{1+t}R+\frac{t}{1+t}Q.a⊗b=1+t1​R+1+tt​Q.
(8)

Taking scalar products with CCC, the optimality of a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b forces both RRR and QQQ to have the same cost as a⊗ba\otimes ba⊗b. Since QQQ was arbitrary, all couplings are optimal.

Thus the product plan is mainly a feasibility witness. Except when the linear cost is constant on the whole transportation polytope, it is not expected to solve optimal transport.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-coupling-polylines")

Discrete couplings represented as straight transport segments. The deterministic graph is a feasible Monge-type plan, the product plan spreads every source mass over all targets, and the optimal Kantorovich plan minimizes the quadratic transport cost. Line width and opacity encode transported mass.

The interactive demo below separates the main feasible-plan archetypes: deterministic graphs, independent product couplings, sparse splitting plans, and entropic approximations.

Interactive panel. Use the point and mass sliders to see how a Kantorovich plan can split mass into several weighted links rather than choosing one destination per source.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-coupling-matrix-marginals")

Coupling matrices with their prescribed marginals. The central grayscale image displays PijP_{ij}Pij​; the red curve on the left is the source marginal aaa, and the blue curve on top is the target marginal bbb. The independent product plan is diffuse, whereas the one-dimensional optimal plan concentrates near the monotone quantile correspondence.

The companion control varies the bin count and the endpoint laws, making the transition from diffuse independence to monotone transport visually explicit.

Interactive panel. Use the problem-size and mass-shape controls to compare the coupling matrix with its red and blue marginal sums.

The Kantorovich feasible set is symmetric: P∈U(a,b)P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)P∈U(a,b) if and only if P⊤∈U(b,a)P^\top\in\CouplingsD(b,a)P⊤∈U(b,a). With a unit transport cost matrix CijC_{ij}Cij​, the discrete Kantorovich problem reads

LC(a,b):=min⁡P∈U(a,b)⟨C,P⟩=min⁡P∈U(a,b)∑i,jCijPij.\mathcal{L}_C(a,b) \eqdef \min_{P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)} \langle C,P\rangle = \min_{P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)} \sum_{i,j} C_{ij}P_{ij}.LC​(a,b):=P∈U(a,b)min​⟨C,P⟩=P∈U(a,b)min​i,j∑​Cij​Pij​.
(9)

This is a linear program, and its solutions need not be unique.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-permutation-versus-splitting")

From permutation matrices to splitting couplings. When the two empirical measures have the same number of atoms and uniform weights, an optimal plan can be a permutation matrix. Once target masses are nonuniform, one source can send mass to several targets and several sources can merge into the same target.

The interactive demo keeps the same source and target sites while changing the target mass imbalance, so the moment where permutation structure breaks becomes visible.

Interactive panel. Use the split-mass and geometry controls to contrast deterministic permutation-like transport with plans that divide source mass across targets.

Proposition: Sparse Optimal Plans

Assume ai>0a_i>0ai​>0, bj>0b_j>0bj​>0 and ∑iai=∑jbj=1\sum_i a_i=\sum_j b_j=1∑i​ai​=∑j​bj​=1. The linear program above admits an optimal coupling with at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 nonzero entries.

Proof

The transportation polytope is compact, so a linear objective attains its minimum at an extreme point. Let PPP be an extreme point and let E={(i,j):Pij>0}E=\{(i,j):P_{ij}>0\}E={(i,j):Pij​>0} be its support graph on the bipartite vertex set of source and target indices. If this graph contains a cycle, put alternating signs +1,−1+1,-1+1,−1 on the cycle, obtaining a nonzero matrix HHH supported on EEE with zero row and column sums. For small t>0t>0t>0, both P+tHP+tHP+tH and P−tHP-tHP−tH are nonnegative couplings and PPP is their midpoint, contradicting extremality. Thus the support graph is a forest, which has at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 edges.

Proposition: North-West Corner Feasible Plan

Let a∈R+na\in\RR_+^na∈R+n​ and b∈R+mb\in\RR_+^mb∈R+m​ have the same positive total mass. A greedy sweep constructs a coupling with at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 positive entries: start at (i,j)=(1,1)(i,j)=(1,1)(i,j)=(1,1) with residual masses ri=air_i=a_iri​=ai​ and sj=bjs_j=b_jsj​=bj​, set

Pij=min⁡(ri,sj),P_{ij}=\min(r_i,s_j),Pij​=min(ri​,sj​),
(10)

subtract this value from both residuals, and advance every index whose residual has become zero. Repeat until all mass is exhausted.

Proof

All assignments are nonnegative. At each step, the mass placed in entry (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) is subtracted from exactly one current row residual and one current column residual, so no row or column can receive more mass than prescribed. Conversely, an index is advanced only when its residual has been fully filled. When the algorithm stops, the total assigned mass is ∑iai=∑jbj\sum_i a_i=\sum_j b_j∑i​ai​=∑j​bj​, hence all row and column sums are exactly aaa and bbb.

Each positive assignment exhausts at least one current row or one current column. Before the final assignment, at most n−1n-1n−1 row advances and m−1m-1m−1 column advances can occur without terminating the construction. Hence the number of positive entries is at most (n−1)+(m−1)+1=n+m−1(n-1)+(m-1)+1=n+m-1(n−1)+(m−1)+1=n+m−1. For acyclicity, view the positive support as a bipartite graph. Once a row or column index is advanced, it never appears again, so each new positive edge either starts a new component or attaches at least one new vertex to the component currently being swept. No edge is ever added between two old vertices of the same component, so no cycle can be created.

Algorithm: North-west corner coupling

Input: Source weights a∈Δn\a\in\simplex_na∈Δn​ and target weights b∈Δm\b\in\simplex_mb∈Δm​.

Output: Sparse feasible coupling P∈U(a,b)\P\in\CouplingsD(\a,\b)P∈U(a,b).

Initialize: Set P=0\P=0P=0, r=ar=\ar=a, s=bs=\bs=b, and (i,j)=(1,1)(i,j)=(1,1)(i,j)=(1,1).

While i≤ni\leq ni≤n and j≤mj\leq mj≤m do:

η=min⁡(ri,sj),Pij←η.\eta=\min(r_i,s_j), \qquad \P_{ij}\leftarrow \eta.η=min(ri​,sj​),Pij​←η.

Update residuals: ri←ri−η,sj←sj−η.r_i\leftarrow r_i-\eta, \qquad s_j\leftarrow s_j-\eta.ri​←ri​−η,sj​←sj​−η.

If ri=0r_i=0ri​=0 then:

Set i←i+1i\leftarrow i+1i←i+1.

If sj=0s_j=0sj​=0 then:

Set j←j+1j\leftarrow j+1j←j+1.

Return P\PP.

The north-west corner rule, summarized in Algorithm Algorithm: North-west corner coupling, does not use the cost matrix and is therefore not meant to solve the discrete Kantorovich problem. Its role is algorithmic: an acyclic support corresponds to linearly independent marginal constraints. When the support has fewer than n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 positive entries, transportation simplex implementations complete it with zero-mass basic variables to obtain a degenerate basic feasible solution. This gives a cheap initialization for the pivoting methods discussed in Section Linear-Programming Algorithms.

One-Dimensional Cases¶

In one dimension, the transportation polytope has a canonical monotone optimizer. This is the weighted version of the sorting rule from the matching chapter.

Proposition: One-Dimensional Weighted Sweep

Let x1≤⋯≤xnx_1\leq\cdots\leq x_nx1​≤⋯≤xn​ and y1≤⋯≤ymy_1\leq\cdots\leq y_my1​≤⋯≤ym​ be points on the line, and let c(x,y)=h(x−y)c(x,y)=h(x-y)c(x,y)=h(x−y) with hhh convex. The north-west corner plan between the sorted weighted atoms is optimal. Consequently, for unsorted one-dimensional inputs, an optimal plan is obtained in O(nlog⁡n+mlog⁡m)O(n\log n+m\log m)O(nlogn+mlogm) time by sorting and then sweeping the masses once from left to right.

Proof

The north-west plan is monotone: it cannot put positive mass on both (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) and (i′,j′)(i',j')(i′,j′) when i<i′i<i'i<i′ and j>j′j>j'j>j′. Conversely, any feasible plan with such a crossing pair can be improved by moving a small mass from (i,j)(i,j)(i,j) and (i′,j′)(i',j')(i′,j′) to (i,j′)(i,j')(i,j′) and (i′,j)(i',j)(i′,j). The two marginals are unchanged, and convexity of hhh gives

h(xi−yj)+h(xi′−yj′)≥h(xi−yj′)+h(xi′−yj)h(x_i-y_j)+h(x_{i'}-y_{j'}) \geq h(x_i-y_{j'})+h(x_{i'}-y_j)h(xi​−yj​)+h(xi′​−yj′​)≥h(xi​−yj′​)+h(xi′​−yj​)
(11)

for i<i′i<i'i<i′ and j′<jj'<jj′<j, with strict inequality for strictly convex hhh and distinct points. Repeating this uncrossing procedure until no crossing remains yields a monotone optimal plan. There is only one monotone feasible plan with the prescribed sorted marginals, namely the sweep plan: it pairs the leftmost remaining source mass with the leftmost remaining target mass at every step. Sorting costs O(nlog⁡n+mlog⁡m)O(n\log n+m\log m)O(nlogn+mlogm) and the sweep uses at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 assignments.

Algorithm: Weighted one-dimensional sweep

Input: One-dimensional atoms (xi,ai)(x_i,\a_i)(xi​,ai​) and (yj,bj)(y_j,\b_j)(yj​,bj​); convex cost h(x−y)h(x-y)h(x−y).

Output: Monotone optimal coupling P\PP.

Sort atoms: x1≤⋯≤xn,y1≤⋯≤ym.x_1\leq\cdots\leq x_n, \qquad y_1\leq\cdots\leq y_m.x1​≤⋯≤xn​,y1​≤⋯≤ym​.

Set P\PP to the output of Algorithm Algorithm: North-west corner coupling applied to the sorted weights (ai)i(\a_i)_i(ai​)i​ and (bj)j(\b_j)_j(bj​)j​. Return P\PP.

Permutation Matrices As Couplings¶

Now assume n=mn=mn=m and uniform weights a=b=1n/na=b=\mathbf{1}_n/na=b=1n​/n. In this case, a matching can be encoded as a matrix with exactly one active entry per row and per column.

Definition: Permutation Matrices

For a permutation σ∈Perm(n)\sigma\in\Perm(n)σ∈Perm(n), its permutation matrix PσP_\sigmaPσ​ is

(Pσ)i,j={1if j=σ(i),0otherwise.(P_\sigma)_{i,j} = \begin{cases} 1 & \text{if } j=\sigma(i),\\ 0 & \text{otherwise}. \end{cases}(Pσ​)i,j​={10​if j=σ(i),otherwise.​
(12)

The set of all permutation matrices is

Pnperm:={Pσ:σ∈Perm(n)}.\mathcal P_n^{\mathrm{perm}} \eqdef \left\{ P_\sigma:\sigma\in\Perm(n) \right\}.Pnperm​:={Pσ​:σ∈Perm(n)}.
(13)

The corresponding probability coupling is Pσ/nP_\sigma/nPσ​/n. If the matching cost matrix is CCC, then

⟨C,Pσ/n⟩=1n∑i=1nCi,σ(i).\langle C,P_\sigma/n\rangle = \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n C_{i,\sigma(i)}.⟨C,Pσ​/n⟩=n1​i=1∑n​Ci,σ(i)​.
(14)

Thus the assignment problem is the minimization of a linear function over the discrete, non-convex set of permutation matrices. The convex relaxation replaces this finite set by all bistochastic matrices.

Definition: Birkhoff Polytope

The Birkhoff polytope is the convex set of bistochastic matrices

Bn:={P∈R+n×n  :  P1n=1n,P⊤1n=1n}.\mathcal B_n \eqdef \left\{ P\in\RR_+^{n\times n} \;:\; P\mathbf{1}_n=\mathbf{1}_n,\quad P^\top\mathbf{1}_n=\mathbf{1}_n \right\}.Bn​:={P∈R+n×n​:P1n​=1n​,P⊤1n​=1n​}.
(15)
Definition: Extreme Points

For a compact convex set C\mathcal CC in a finite-dimensional vector space,

Extr⁡(C):={x∈C:x=(y+z)/2, y,z∈C⇒y=z=x}.\Extr(\mathcal C) \eqdef \{x\in\mathcal C: x=(y+z)/2,\ y,z\in\mathcal C \Rightarrow y=z=x\}.Extr(C):={x∈C:x=(y+z)/2, y,z∈C⇒y=z=x}.
(16)
Proposition: Existence of Extreme Points

If C\mathcal CC is a nonempty compact convex subset of a finite-dimensional vector space, then Extr⁡(C)\Extr(\mathcal C)Extr(C) is nonempty.

Proof

Among all nonempty faces of C\mathcal CC, choose one of minimal affine dimension. If this face contained two distinct points, maximizing a linear functional that is not constant on the face would produce a nonempty proper exposed subface, contradicting minimality. Hence the minimal face is a singleton, and its point is extreme.

Example: Unbounded convex sets may have no extreme point

Compactness cannot be dropped from Proposition Proposition: Existence of Extreme Points. For instance, the closed convex set {(x,y)∈R+2  :  xy≥1}\enscond{(x,y)\in\RR_+^2}{xy\geq1}{(x,y)∈R+2​:xy≥1} is unbounded and has no extreme point.

Proposition: Linear Programs Have Extreme Minimizers

Let C\mathcal CC be nonempty and compact. For every linear form ℓ\ellℓ,

Extr⁡(C)∩arg min⁡x∈Cℓ(x)≠∅.\Extr(\mathcal C)\cap\argmin_{x\in\mathcal C}\ell(x)\neq\emptyset.Extr(C)∩x∈Cargmin​ℓ(x)=∅.
(17)
Proof

The set S=arg min⁡x∈Cℓ(x)S=\argmin_{x\in\mathcal C}\ell(x)S=argminx∈C​ℓ(x) is nonempty, compact and convex. By Proposition Proposition: Existence of Extreme Points, it has an extreme point xxx. If x=(y+z)/2x=(y+z)/2x=(y+z)/2 with y,z∈Cy,z\in\mathcal Cy,z∈C, then by linearity and optimality of xxx, both yyy and zzz also minimize ℓ\ellℓ on C\mathcal CC, hence y,z∈Sy,z\in Sy,z∈S. Since xxx is extreme in SSS, y=z=xy=z=xy=z=x. Thus xxx is extreme in C\mathcal CC.

Theorem: Birkhoff--von Neumann

The extreme points of Bn\mathcal B_nBn​ are exactly the permutation matrices.

Figure Div shows the non-extreme mechanism used in the proof below. The displayed matrix is bistochastic but not a permutation matrix: the unit entries already behave like isolated matching edges, while the fractional support contains a minimal alternating cycle.

show_book_figure("birkhoff-von-neumann-cycle")

Cycle certificate in the Birkhoff--von Neumann proof. The left panel is a 7×77\times77×7 bistochastic matrix which is not a permutation matrix. The right panel shows its bipartite positive-support graph, with the column nodes ordered for readability: red nodes are rows, blue nodes are columns, thin purple edges correspond to 0<Pij<10<P_{ij}<10<Pij​<1, and bold black edges correspond to isolated entries Pij=1P_{ij}=1Pij​=1. The orange halo marks the minimal 2×22\times22×2 fractional cycle along which one can add and subtract mass while preserving all row and column sums.

Proof

We first prove that permutation matrices are extreme. Let Pσ∈PnpermP_\sigma\in\mathcal P_n^{\mathrm{perm}}Pσ​∈Pnperm​ and assume that

Pσ=Q+R2withQ,R∈Bn.P_\sigma=\frac{Q+R}{2} \qquad\text{with}\qquad Q,R\in\mathcal B_n .Pσ​=2Q+R​withQ,R∈Bn​.
(18)

Every bistochastic matrix has entries in [0,1][0,1][0,1]. Since the only extreme points of [0,1][0,1][0,1] are 0 and 1, each entry of PσP_\sigmaPσ​ fixes the corresponding entries of QQQ and RRR: if (Pσ)ij=0(P_\sigma)_{ij}=0(Pσ​)ij​=0, then Qij=Rij=0Q_{ij}=R_{ij}=0Qij​=Rij​=0, while if (Pσ)ij=1(P_\sigma)_{ij}=1(Pσ​)ij​=1, then Qij=Rij=1Q_{ij}=R_{ij}=1Qij​=Rij​=1. Hence Q=R=PσQ=R=P_\sigmaQ=R=Pσ​, so PσP_\sigmaPσ​ is extreme.

We now prove the converse by contrapositive. Pick P∈Bn∖PnpermP\in\mathcal B_n\setminus\mathcal P_n^{\mathrm{perm}}P∈Bn​∖Pnperm​. Since an integral bistochastic matrix is necessarily a permutation matrix, PPP has at least one fractional entry. We shall split P=(Q+R)/2P=(Q+R)/2P=(Q+R)/2 with Q,R∈BnQ,R\in\mathcal B_nQ,R∈Bn​ and Q≠RQ\neq RQ=R, proving that PPP is not extreme.

Associate with PPP the bipartite graph whose left vertices are the rows, whose right vertices are the columns, and whose edges are the fractional entries 0<Pij<10<P_{ij}<10<Pij​<1. An entry equal to 1 uses the whole mass of its row and column, so it is isolated in the positive support and does not appear in this fractional graph. If a left vertex is incident to one fractional edge, then it must be incident to at least one other fractional edge: after the first fractional contribution, the row still has positive remaining mass, and that remainder cannot be carried by an entry equal to 1. The same argument applies to columns. Thus every non-isolated vertex of the fractional graph has degree at least two.

Starting from any fractional edge, one may therefore walk through adjacent fractional edges without immediately backtracking and without getting stuck. Since the graph is finite, some vertex is eventually visited twice; the portion of the walk between the two visits contains a cycle. Choose a shortest such cycle and write it in alternating form

(i1,j1,i2,j2,…,ip,jp),ip+1=i1,(i_1,j_1,i_2,j_2,\ldots,i_p,j_p), \qquad i_{p+1}=i_1,(i1​,j1​,i2​,j2​,…,ip​,jp​),ip+1​=i1​,
(19)

where both (is,js)(i_s,j_s)(is​,js​) and (is+1,js)(i_{s+1},j_s)(is+1​,js​) are fractional for every sss. Define

ϵ:=min⁡1≤s≤p{Pis,js,Pis+1,js,1−Pis,js,1−Pis+1,js}>0,\epsilon \eqdef \min_{1\leq s\leq p} \{ P_{i_s,j_s}, P_{i_{s+1},j_s}, 1-P_{i_s,j_s}, 1-P_{i_{s+1},j_s} \}>0,ϵ:=1≤s≤pmin​{Pis​,js​​,Pis+1​,js​​,1−Pis​,js​​,1−Pis+1​,js​​}>0,
(20)

and split the cycle edges into the alternating families

A={(is,js)}s=1p,B={(is+1,js)}s=1p.A=\{(i_s,j_s)\}_{s=1}^p, \qquad B=\{(i_{s+1},j_s)\}_{s=1}^p .A={(is​,js​)}s=1p​,B={(is+1​,js​)}s=1p​.
(21)

Set Q=PQ=PQ=P and R=PR=PR=P outside A∪BA\cup BA∪B; on AAA, set Qij=Pij+ϵ/2Q_{ij}=P_{ij}+\epsilon/2Qij​=Pij​+ϵ/2 and Rij=Pij−ϵ/2R_{ij}=P_{ij}-\epsilon/2Rij​=Pij​−ϵ/2; on BBB, set Qij=Pij−ϵ/2Q_{ij}=P_{ij}-\epsilon/2Qij​=Pij​−ϵ/2 and Rij=Pij+ϵ/2R_{ij}=P_{ij}+\epsilon/2Rij​=Pij​+ϵ/2. By the definition of ϵ\epsilonϵ, all modified entries stay in [0,1][0,1][0,1]. Each row and column of the cycle sees one +ϵ/2+\epsilon/2+ϵ/2 and one −ϵ/2-\epsilon/2−ϵ/2, so the row and column sums remain one. Thus Q,R∈BnQ,R\in\mathcal B_nQ,R∈Bn​, Q≠RQ\neq RQ=R, and P=(Q+R)/2P=(Q+R)/2P=(Q+R)/2. Hence PPP is not extreme. Consequently every extreme point of Bn\mathcal B_nBn​ is integral, and every integral bistochastic matrix is a permutation matrix.

The same combinatorial idea gives the constructive decomposition used to express a bistochastic matrix as a convex combination of permutations.

Algorithm: Birkhoff--von Neumann decomposition

Input: Bistochastic matrix P∈BnP\in\mathcal B_nP∈Bn​.

Output: Decomposition P=∑rλrPσrP=\sum_r\lambda_rP_{\sigma_r}P=∑r​λr​Pσr​​.

Initialize: Set R=PR=PR=P and L=∅\mathcal L=\emptysetL=∅.

While R≠0R\neq0R=0 do:

Build bipartite graph GR={(i,j):Rij>0}G_R=\{(i,j):R_{ij}>0\}GR​={(i,j):Rij​>0}.

Set σ\sigmaσ to the lexicographically first perfect matching of GRG_RGR​.

Set λ=min⁡iRi,σ(i).\lambda=\min_i R_{i,\sigma(i)}.λ=mini​Ri,σ(i)​.

Append (λ,σ)(\lambda,\sigma)(λ,σ) to L\mathcal LL.

Update R←R−λPσ.R\leftarrow R-\lambda P_\sigma .R←R−λPσ​.

Return P=∑(λr,σr)∈LλrPσr,∑rλr=1.P=\sum_{(\lambda_r,\sigma_r)\in\mathcal L}\lambda_rP_{\sigma_r}, \qquad \sum_r\lambda_r=1.P=∑(λr​,σr​)∈L​λr​Pσr​​,∑r​λr​=1.

Corollary: Kantorovich For Matching

If m=nm=nm=n and a=b=1n/n\a=\b=\ones_n/na=b=1n​/n, then the discrete Kantorovich problem admits an optimal solution of the form Pσ/nP_\sigma/nPσ​/n. The associated permutation σ\sigmaσ solves the assignment problem.

Proof

The feasible set is Bn/n\mathcal B_n/nBn​/n. By Proposition Proposition: Linear Programs Have Extreme Minimizers, the linear objective has an optimal extreme point. Since scaling preserves extreme points and Theorem Theorem: Birkhoff--von Neumann identifies the extreme points of Bn\mathcal B_nBn​, this optimizer is Pσ/nP_\sigma/nPσ​/n for some permutation σ\sigmaσ. Its cost is exactly n−1∑iCi,σ(i)n^{-1}\sum_i C_{i,\sigma(i)}n−1∑i​Ci,σ(i)​, so σ\sigmaσ is an optimal assignment.

Equivalently, for uniform empirical measures, one can always choose a permutation matrix among the minimizers of the relaxed Kantorovich problem: the relaxation is tight for assignment problems.

Remark: General discrete case

For general input measures, one does not have equivalence between Monge and Kantorovich problems, since the Monge constraint can be empty. In finite dimension, however, the support of an optimal coupling still enjoys strong sparsity: one can choose an optimal basic feasible plan whose bipartite support is cycle-free, hence with at most n+m−1n+m-1n+m−1 nonzero entries. Figure Div illustrates the difference between the tight uniform matching case and the genuinely splitting nonuniform case.

Linear-Programming Algorithms¶

The discrete Kantorovich problem is a linear program with much more structure than a generic dense LP. Its variables are arcs of a complete bipartite network, its equality constraints are flow-conservation constraints, and its extreme points are sparse tree-like couplings.

Transportation Simplex And Network Simplex¶

The transportation simplex goes back to Dantzig’s formulation of the transportation problem Dantzig, 1951. It works on basic feasible couplings, whose support is completed into a spanning tree of the bipartite supply-demand graph. Reduced costs identify whether an unused arc can decrease the objective. Adding such an arc creates a unique cycle; one then pushes as much mass as possible around that cycle and removes the exhausted arc.

The network simplex is the corresponding pivoting method for general minimum-cost-flow problems Bertsekas & Eckstein, 1988. It keeps node potentials, reduced costs and a spanning-tree basis. Its worst-case number of pivots can be exponential, but the per-pivot operations exploit graph sparsity. Polynomial guarantees can be obtained from strongly polynomial minimum-cost-flow algorithms such as Orlin’s algorithm Orlin, 1997.

Interior-Point Methods¶

Generic interior-point methods approach the LP through a smooth central path. For the transport polytope, the logarithmic-barrier version is

Pϵ:=arg min⁡P1m=a,P⊤1n=bPij>0⟨C,P⟩−ϵ∑i,jlog⁡Pij.P_\epsilon \eqdef \argmin_{\substack{P\mathbf{1}_m=a,\;P^\top\mathbf{1}_n=b\\P_{ij}>0}} \langle C,P\rangle - \epsilon\sum_{i,j}\log P_{ij}.Pϵ​:=P1m​=a,P⊤1n​=bPij​>0​argmin​⟨C,P⟩−ϵi,j∑​logPij​.
(22)

The barrier is singular at the boundary, so each iterate stays strictly inside the transportation polytope. As ϵ↓0\epsilon\downarrow0ϵ↓0, the central path approaches the set of LP minimizers.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-log-barrier-lp-geometry")

Logarithmic-barrier central path for a triangular slice of a linear program. Large ϵ\epsilonϵ selects a central interior point; decreasing ϵ\epsilonϵ moves the minimizer toward the optimal vertex while never touching the boundary. This differs from entropic OT, where the entropy temperature is part of the regularized objective itself.

The interactive view exposes the barrier parameter directly: lowering ϵ\epsilonϵ slides the minimizer from the center of the feasible triangle toward the LP vertex.

Interactive panel. Use the barrier and angle controls to move along the interior central path of the transport polytope.

Both interior-point methods and Sinkhorn keep iterates positive, but they use positivity differently. Interior-point algorithms solve the original LP by decreasing a barrier parameter. Sinkhorn fixes an entropic temperature and solves a different, KL-regularized OT problem by alternating diagonal scalings.

Relaxation For Arbitrary Measures¶

This section lifts the finite-dimensional coupling matrix to a joint probability measure. The payoff is that existence, duality and metric properties can be stated for arbitrary laws, including discrete, singular and continuous distributions.

Continuous Couplings¶

Definition: Marginals Of A Joint Measure

Let π∈M+1(X×Y)\pi\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx\times\Yy)π∈M+1​(X×Y) and let PX(x,y)=xP_\Xx(x,y)=xPX​(x,y)=x and PY(x,y)=yP_\Yy(x,y)=yPY​(x,y)=y be the coordinate projections. The marginals of π\piπ are

π1:=(PX)♯π,π2:=(PY)♯π.\pi_1\eqdef(P_\Xx)_\sharp\pi, \qquad \pi_2\eqdef(P_\Yy)_\sharp\pi.π1​:=(PX​)♯​π,π2​:=(PY​)♯​π.
(23)

Equivalently, for bounded continuous test functions fff on X\XxX and ggg on Y\YyY,

∫f(x) dπ(x,y)=∫f dπ1,∫g(y) dπ(x,y)=∫g dπ2.\int f(x)\d\pi(x,y)=\int f\d\pi_1, \qquad \int g(y)\d\pi(x,y)=\int g\d\pi_2.∫f(x)dπ(x,y)=∫fdπ1​,∫g(y)dπ(x,y)=∫gdπ2​.
(24)
Definition: Couplings

Given α∈M+1(X)\al\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx)α∈M+1​(X) and β∈M+1(Y)\be\in\Mm_+^1(\Yy)β∈M+1​(Y), the set of couplings between α\alα and β\beβ is

Π(α,β):={π∈M+1(X×Y):π1=α, π2=β}.\Couplings(\al,\be) \eqdef \{\pi\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx\times\Yy):\pi_1=\al,\ \pi_2=\be\}.Π(α,β):={π∈M+1​(X×Y):π1​=α, π2​=β}.
(25)

This is the continuous analogue of the transportation polytope.

Remark: Probabilistic interpretation of couplings

If X∼αX\sim\alX∼α and Y∼βY\sim\beY∼β, then π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β) means that π\piπ is the law of a pair (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y) whose coordinates have laws α\alα and β\beβ. The coupling encodes the dependence between XXX and YYY. The tensor product α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β corresponds to independence, whereas a graph coupling (Id,T)♯α(\Id,T)_\sharp\al(Id,T)♯​α corresponds to the deterministic relation Y=T(X)Y=T(X)Y=T(X).

In the discrete case, when α=∑iaiδxi\al=\sum_i \a_i\de_{x_i}α=∑i​ai​δxi​​ and β=∑jbjδyj\be=\sum_j \b_j\de_{y_j}β=∑j​bj​δyj​​, the constraint π1=α\pi_1=\alπ1​=α and π2=β\pi_2=\beπ2​=β forces every coupling to have the form π=∑i,jPijδ(xi,yj)\pi=\sum_{i,j}\P_{ij}\de_{(x_i,y_j)}π=∑i,j​Pij​δ(xi​,yj​)​ with P∈U(a,b)\P\in\CouplingsD(\a,\b)P∈U(a,b). The discrete formulation is therefore a special case of the continuous one, not merely an approximation.

Unlike the Monge constraint, the coupling constraint is never empty. The continuous feasibility witness is the tensor product coupling.

Definition: Tensor Product And Trivial Coupling

Given α∈M+1(X)\al\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx)α∈M+1​(X) and β∈M+1(Y)\be\in\Mm_+^1(\Yy)β∈M+1​(Y), the tensor product coupling α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β is defined by

∫h(x,y) d(α⊗β)(x,y)=∫X(∫Yh(x,y) dβ(y)) dα(x).\int h(x,y)\d(\al\otimes\be)(x,y) = \int_\Xx \left(\int_\Yy h(x,y)\d\be(y)\right) \d\al(x).∫h(x,y)d(α⊗β)(x,y)=∫X​(∫Y​h(x,y)dβ(y))dα(x).
(26)

The next result echoes Proposition Proposition: Discrete Product Optimality Is Degenerate in the continuous setting. In both cases, the independent coupling is optimal precisely when the objective is flat over the whole admissible set; for continuous costs, this flatness is equivalently the additive form c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y) on the product support.

Proposition: Product Optimality Is Degenerate

Assume that X\XxX and Y\YyY are compact metric spaces and that c∈C(X×Y)c\in\Cc(\Xx\times\Yy)c∈C(X×Y). The tensor product α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β is optimal for inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫c dπ\inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)}\int c\d\piinfπ∈Π(α,β)​∫cdπ if and only if every coupling is optimal. This is also equivalent to the additive decomposition

c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)c(x,y)=u(x)+v(y)
(27)

on the product support.

Proof Sketch

If all couplings are optimal, the product coupling is optimal. Conversely, assume the product is optimal. If cross differences failed to vanish on the product support, there would be points x0,x1,y0,y1x_0,x_1,y_0,y_1x0​,x1​,y0​,y1​ such that exchanging the two target neighborhoods decreases cost. Replacing a small amount of product mass on the two diagonal rectangles by mass on the crossed rectangles keeps the same marginals and lowers the cost, a contradiction. Vanishing cross differences imply c(x,y)=c(x,y⋆)+c(x⋆,y)−c(x⋆,y⋆)c(x,y)=c(x,y_\star)+c(x_\star,y)-c(x_\star,y_\star)c(x,y)=c(x,y⋆​)+c(x⋆​,y)−c(x⋆​,y⋆​) on the support, so the cost of any coupling depends only on its marginals.

If there exists a map T:X→YT:\Xx\to\YyT:X→Y with T♯α=βT_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β, then the Monge map induces the graph coupling π=(Id,T)♯α∈Π(α,β)\pi=(\Id,T)_\sharp\al\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π=(Id,T)♯​α∈Π(α,β), characterized by

∫h(x,y) dπ(x,y)=∫h(x,T(x)) dα(x).\int h(x,y)\d\pi(x,y) = \int h(x,T(x))\d\al(x).∫h(x,y)dπ(x,y)=∫h(x,T(x))dα(x).
(28)

Graph couplings are precisely the Kantorovich representation of deterministic Monge maps.

Continuous Kantorovich Problem¶

The discrete Kantorovich problem becomes, for arbitrary measures,

Lc(α,β):=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫X×Yc(x,y) dπ(x,y).\mathcal{L}_c(\al,\be) \eqdef \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)} \int_{\Xx\times\Yy} c(x,y)\d\pi(x,y).Lc​(α,β):=π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫X×Y​c(x,y)dπ(x,y).
(29)

This is an infinite-dimensional linear program over a space of measures.

Remark: Probabilistic Interpretation

The same problem can be written as

Lc(α,β)=inf⁡X∼α,  Y∼βE c(X,Y).\mathcal{L}_c(\al,\be) = \inf_{X\sim\al,\;Y\sim\be} \mathbb{E}\,c(X,Y).Lc​(α,β)=X∼α,Y∼βinf​Ec(X,Y).
(30)

The minimization is over all possible dependences between the two random variables, not over the fixed marginal laws.

Proposition: Existence On Compact Spaces

Assume that X\XxX and Y\YyY are compact metric spaces and c∈C(X×Y)c\in\Cc(\Xx\times\Yy)c∈C(X×Y). Then the Kantorovich problem admits at least one minimizer.

Proof

The constraint set is nonempty because it contains α⊗β\al\otimes\beα⊗β. It is closed for weak convergence because the marginal constraints are preserved. Let Ω:=X×Y\Omega\eqdef\Xx\times\YyΩ:=X×Y. Since Ω\OmegaΩ is compact, Proposition Proposition: Wasserstein Spaces As Ground Spaces gives compactness of P(Ω)\Pp(\Omega)P(Ω) for the Wasserstein topology. On compact spaces this topology is the weak topology by Proposition Proposition: Wasserstein Metrizes Weak Convergence On Compact Spaces; hence Π(α,β)\Couplings(\al,\be)Π(α,β) is compact. Finally, π↦∫c dπ\pi\mapsto\int c\d\piπ↦∫cdπ is weakly continuous because ccc is continuous and bounded.

On non-compact domains, one needs coercivity and moment conditions. For the Wasserstein cost c(x,y)=d(x,y)pc(x,y)=d(x,y)^pc(x,y)=d(x,y)p on a Polish metric space, the natural domain is

Pp(X):={μ∈M+1(X):∫d(x,x0)p dμ(x)<+∞},\mathcal P_p(\Xx) \eqdef \left\{ \mu\in\Mm_+^1(\Xx): \int d(x,x_0)^p\d\mu(x)<+\infty \right\},Pp​(X):={μ∈M+1​(X):∫d(x,x0​)pdμ(x)<+∞},
(31)

for one, and hence every, reference point x0x_0x0​.

Monge--Kantorovich Equivalence¶

The proof of Brenier’s theorem relies on Kantorovich relaxation and duality. Under Brenier’s hypotheses, the relaxation is tight: it has the same cost as the Monge problem and the optimal coupling is induced by a map.

Corollary: Monge--Kantorovich Equivalence Under Brenier

Assume that α\alα is absolutely continuous with respect to Lebesgue measure and that c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\|x-y\|^2c(x,y)=∥x−y∥2. If TTT is the Brenier map solving Monge’s problem, then π=(Id,T)♯α\pi=(\Id,T)_\sharp\alπ=(Id,T)♯​α is the unique optimal coupling solving Kantorovich’s problem. In particular, the Monge and Kantorovich costs are the same.

Proof

The proof of Brenier’s theorem shows that the support of any optimal Kantorovich plan lies in the subdifferential ∂ϕ\partial\phi∂ϕ of a convex function. Since α\alα has a density, ϕ\phiϕ is differentiable α\alα-almost everywhere, so ∂ϕ(x)={∇ϕ(x)}\partial\phi(x)=\{\nabla\phi(x)\}∂ϕ(x)={∇ϕ(x)} for α\alα-almost every xxx. Every optimal coupling is therefore concentrated on the graph of T=∇ϕT=\nabla\phiT=∇ϕ and equals (Id,T)♯α(\Id,T)_\sharp\al(Id,T)♯​α.

If α\alα does not have a density, non-smooth points of ϕ\phiϕ can be charged by α\alα and mass splitting can occur. For instance, moving δ0\delta_0δ0​ to (δ−1+δ1)/2(\delta_{-1}+\delta_1)/2(δ−1​+δ1​)/2 can be represented by a plan concentrated on the set-valued subdifferential of ϕ(x)=∣x∣\phi(x)=|x|ϕ(x)=∣x∣, but not by a deterministic map.

Remark: Probabilistic interpretation of Kantorovich’s problem

The same problem can be written as

Lc(α,β)=inf⁡X∼α, Y∼βE(c(X,Y)).\MK_c(\al,\be) = \inf_{X\sim\al,\,Y\sim\be}\EE(c(X,Y)).Lc​(α,β)=X∼α,Y∼βinf​E(c(X,Y)).
(32)

The minimization is not over the marginal laws, which are fixed, but over all possible dependences between the two random variables. OT therefore chooses the cheapest joint law among all couplings.

Remark: Nonsmooth potentials and splitting

If α\alα does not have a density, then ϕ\phiϕ may be non-smooth on a set charged by α\alα, and non-smooth points can lead to mass splitting. For instance, moving δ0\delta_0δ0​ to (δ−1+δ+1)/2(\delta_{-1}+\delta_{+1})/2(δ−1​+δ+1​)/2 can be represented by a plan concentrated on the set-valued subdifferential of ϕ(x)=∣x∣\phi(x)=|x|ϕ(x)=∣x∣, but not by a deterministic map. This is the continuous counterpart of the gap between the uniform matching case of Corollary Corollary: Kantorovich For Matching and the general splitting case.

Remark: Probabilistic form of tightness

If (X,Y)(X,Y)(X,Y) has the optimal Kantorovich law under the assumptions of Corollary Corollary: Monge--Kantorovich Equivalence Under Brenier, then Y=T(X)Y=T(X)Y=T(X) almost surely with X∼αX\sim\alX∼α and T(X)∼βT(X)\sim\beT(X)∼β. This is analogous to the Birkhoff--von Neumann result in the fully discrete uniform case: in both settings, the convex relaxation admits an optimizer satisfying the original deterministic constraint. The hypotheses are quite different, however: Birkhoff--von Neumann is finite-dimensional and need not give uniqueness, whereas Brenier’s theorem uses absolute continuity of the source and gives uniqueness of the optimal map almost everywhere.

Remark: Book-shifting as a flat Kantorovich face

The Monge book-shifting example in Example Example: Book-shifting in the Monge problem has a transparent coupling interpretation. Let α\alα be uniform on [0,2][0,2][0,2] and β\beβ uniform on [1,3][1,3][1,3]. For every π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β),

∫∣y−x∣ dπ(x,y)≥∫(y−x) dπ(x,y)=∫y dβ(y)−∫x dα(x)=1.\int |y-x|\d\pi(x,y) \geq \int (y-x)\d\pi(x,y) = \int y\d\be(y)-\int x\d\al(x) =1.∫∣y−x∣dπ(x,y)≥∫(y−x)dπ(x,y)=∫ydβ(y)−∫xdα(x)=1.
(33)

Equality holds exactly for couplings concentrated on the half-plane {(x,y):y≥x}\{(x,y):y\geq x\}{(x,y):y≥x}, where ∣y−x∣=y−x|y-x|=y-x∣y−x∣=y−x. Hence the optimal set is a whole face of the coupling polytope, not a single graph. The translation and book-shifting maps give two graph couplings inside this face, but many non-deterministic couplings are optimal as well.

Cyclical Monotonicity¶

Cyclical monotonicity is the local geometric fingerprint of optimality for a cost ccc. It converts a global minimization problem into finite exchange inequalities and is the bridge from Kantorovich plans to convex potentials.

Definition: Support

For a Radon measure π\piπ on X×Y\Xx\times\YyX×Y,

supp⁡(π):={(x,y):π(U×V)>0 for every open U∋x, V∋y}.\supp(\pi) \eqdef \{(x,y):\pi(U\times V)>0 \text{ for every open }U\ni x,\ V\ni y\}.supp(π):={(x,y):π(U×V)>0 for every open U∋x, V∋y}.
(34)
Definition: ccc-Cyclical Monotonicity

A set Γ⊂X×Y\Gamma\subset\Xx\times\YyΓ⊂X×Y is ccc-cyclically monotone if, for every k≥2k\geq2k≥2, every finite family (xi,yi)i=1k⊂Γ(x_i,y_i)_{i=1}^k\subset\Gamma(xi​,yi​)i=1k​⊂Γ and every permutation σ\sigmaσ of {1,…,k}\{1,\ldots,k\}{1,…,k},

∑i=1kc(xi,yi)≤∑i=1kc(xi,yσ(i)).\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i) \leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{\sigma(i)}).i=1∑k​c(xi​,yi​)≤i=1∑k​c(xi​,yσ(i)​).
(35)

It is enough to check cyclic permutations:

∑i=1kc(xi,yi)≤∑i=1kc(xi,yi+1),yk+1=y1.\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_i) \leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,y_{i+1}), \qquad y_{k+1}=y_1.i=1∑k​c(xi​,yi​)≤i=1∑k​c(xi​,yi+1​),yk+1​=y1​.
(36)
Theorem: Optimal Plans Are ccc-Cyclically Monotone

Assume ccc is continuous. For any optimal plan π\piπ solving the Kantorovich problem, supp⁡(π)\supp(\pi)supp(π) is ccc-cyclically monotone.

Proof Sketch

Suppose a finite family in supp⁡(π)\supp(\pi)supp(π) violates the exchange inequality. By continuity, the same strict inequality holds in small neighborhoods Ui×ViU_i\times V_iUi​×Vi​ around the chosen pairs. Remove a small equal amount of mass from each rectangle, keep its first and second marginal pieces, and reinsert the mass after permuting the second marginal pieces. The new measure has the same marginals but strictly smaller cost, contradicting optimality.

If the optimal plan is induced by a map TTT, cyclical monotonicity reads

∑i=1kc(xi,T(xi))≤∑i=1kc(xi,T(xi+1)).\sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,T(x_i)) \leq \sum_{i=1}^k c(x_i,T(x_{i+1})).i=1∑k​c(xi​,T(xi​))≤i=1∑k​c(xi​,T(xi+1​)).
(37)

For c(x,y)=12∥x−y∥2c(x,y)=\frac12\|x-y\|^2c(x,y)=21​∥x−y∥2, the two-point case gives

⟨T(x)−T(y),x−y⟩≥0,\langle T(x)-T(y),x-y\rangle\geq0,⟨T(x)−T(y),x−y⟩≥0,
(38)

so TTT is a monotone vector field. In one dimension, for c(x,y)=∣x−y∣pc(x,y)=|x-y|^pc(x,y)=∣x−y∣p, this reduces to the classical monotone rearrangement.

Metric Properties: Wasserstein Distances¶

OT costs become genuine distances when the ground cost comes from a metric. The proof relies on a gluing lemma.

Lemma: Discrete Gluing Lemma

Given a∈Δna\in\simplex_na∈Δn​, b∈Δpb\in\simplex_pb∈Δp​, c∈Δmc\in\simplex_mc∈Δm​, P∈U(a,b)P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)P∈U(a,b) and Q∈U(b,c)Q\in\CouplingsD(b,c)Q∈U(b,c), define

R=Pdiag⁡(1/b)Q,Rik=∑j:bj>0PijQjkbj.R=P\diag(1/b)Q, \qquad R_{ik}=\sum_{j:b_j>0}\frac{P_{ij}Q_{jk}}{b_j}.R=Pdiag(1/b)Q,Rik​=j:bj​>0∑​bj​Pij​Qjk​​.
(39)

Then R∈U(a,c)R\in\CouplingsD(a,c)R∈U(a,c). It is the first-third marginal of the tensor coupling

Sijk={PijQjk/bj,bj>0,0,bj=0.S_{ijk} = \begin{cases} P_{ij}Q_{jk}/b_j, & b_j>0,\\ 0, & b_j=0. \end{cases}Sijk​={Pij​Qjk​/bj​,0,​bj​>0,bj​=0.​
(40)
Proof

If bj>0b_j>0bj​>0, summing SijkS_{ijk}Sijk​ over kkk gives Pijbj/bj=PijP_{ij}b_j/b_j=P_{ij}Pij​bj​/bj​=Pij​; if bj=0b_j=0bj​=0, the corresponding column of PPP and row of QQQ are zero. The other prescribed marginal is checked in the same way. Summing over the intermediate index jjj gives RRR. Its row and column sums are aaa and ccc.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-discrete-gluing-lemma")

Discrete gluing lemma in matrix form. The first two panels are optimal one-dimensional couplings through an intermediate marginal. The third panel shows the induced marginal R=Pdiag⁡(1/b)QR=P\diag(1/b)QR=Pdiag(1/b)Q; it is feasible and is the coupling used in the triangle-inequality proof.

The interactive version changes the resolution of the intermediate marginal, which controls how mediated the glued source-target plan becomes.

Interactive panel. Use the mediation slider to inspect how two couplings through an intermediate marginal glue into a source-target plan.

Definition: Discrete Wasserstein Distance

Let DDD be a distance matrix on {1,…,n}\{1,\ldots,n\}{1,…,n} and p≥1p\geq1p≥1. The discrete ppp-Wasserstein distance between histograms is

Wp(a,b):=(min⁡P∈U(a,b)∑i,jDijpPij)1/pW_p(a,b)\eqdef \left( \min_{P\in\CouplingsD(a,b)} \sum_{i,j} D_{ij}^p P_{ij} \right)^{1/p}Wp​(a,b):=(P∈U(a,b)min​i,j∑​Dijp​Pij​)1/p
(41)

It depends on the chosen ground distance DDD.

Proposition: Metric Property Of Discrete Wasserstein Distance

For every distance matrix DDD on {1,…,n}\{1,\ldots,n\}{1,…,n}, Definition Definition: Discrete Wasserstein Distance defines a distance on Δn\simplex_nΔn​: WpW_pWp​ is symmetric, positive, Wp(a,b)=0W_p(a,b)=0Wp​(a,b)=0 if and only if a=ba=ba=b, and it satisfies the triangle inequality.

Proof

Symmetry follows by transposing couplings. Positivity follows because a zero cost plan must be supported on the diagonal. For the triangle inequality, take optimal couplings PPP from aaa to bbb and QQQ from bbb to ccc, glue them into SSS, and use the feasible marginal RRR from aaa to ccc. Then Minkowski’s inequality and the ground triangle inequality give

Wp(a,c)≤(∑i,j,k(Dij+Djk)pSijk)1/p≤Wp(a,b)+Wp(b,c).W_p(a,c) \leq \left(\sum_{i,j,k}(D_{ij}+D_{jk})^pS_{ijk}\right)^{1/p} \leq W_p(a,b)+W_p(b,c).Wp​(a,c)≤⎝⎛​i,j,k∑​(Dij​+Djk​)pSijk​⎠⎞​1/p≤Wp​(a,b)+Wp​(b,c).
(42)
Lemma: Gluing Lemma

Let (α,β,γ)(\al,\be,\ga)(α,β,γ) be probability measures on Polish spaces. Given π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β) and ξ∈Π(β,γ)\xi\in\Couplings(\be,\ga)ξ∈Π(β,γ), there exists a measure σ\sigmaσ on X×Y×Z\Xx\times\Yy\times\ZzX×Y×Z such that

(PX,Y)♯σ=π,(PY,Z)♯σ=ξ.(P_{\Xx,\Yy})_\sharp\sigma=\pi, \qquad (P_{\Yy,\Zz})_\sharp\sigma=\xi.(PX,Y​)♯​σ=π,(PY,Z​)♯​σ=ξ.
(43)
Proof Sketch

Disintegrate π\piπ and ξ\xiξ against their common marginal β\beβ, obtaining conditional laws πy\pi_yπy​ on X\XxX and ξy\xi_yξy​ on Z\ZzZ. Define σ\sigmaσ by the conditional-product formula

∫g(x,y,z) dσ=∫Y(∫X∫Zg(x,y,z) dπy(x) dξy(z)) dβ(y).\int g(x,y,z)\d\sigma = \int_\Yy \left(\int_\Xx\int_\Zz g(x,y,z)\d\pi_y(x)\d\xi_y(z)\right) \d\be(y).∫g(x,y,z)dσ=∫Y​(∫X​∫Z​g(x,y,z)dπy​(x)dξy​(z))dβ(y).
(44)

This is the measure-theoretic version of the discrete formula above.

Definition: Wasserstein Distance

Let (X,d)(\X,d)(X,d) be a metric space and p≥1p\geq1p≥1. For α,β∈Pp(X)\al,\be\in\Pp_p(\X)α,β∈Pp​(X), the ppp-Wasserstein distance is

Wp(α,β):=(inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)∫d(x,y)p dπ(x,y))1/p\Wass_p(\al,\be) \eqdef \left( \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)} \int d(x,y)^p\d\pi(x,y) \right)^{1/p}Wp​(α,β):=(π∈Π(α,β)inf​∫d(x,y)pdπ(x,y))1/p
(45)

It depends on the ground distance ddd.

Proposition: Metric Property Of The Wasserstein Distance

Definition Definition: Wasserstein Distance defines a distance: Wp\Wass_pWp​ is symmetric, positive, Wp(α,β)=0\Wass_p(\al,\be)=0Wp​(α,β)=0 if and only if α=β\al=\beα=β, and it satisfies the triangle inequality

∀(α,β,γ)∈Pp(X)3,Wp(α,γ)≤Wp(α,β)+Wp(β,γ).\forall(\al,\be,\ga)\in\Pp_p(\X)^3, \qquad \Wass_p(\al,\ga) \leq \Wass_p(\al,\be)+\Wass_p(\be,\ga).∀(α,β,γ)∈Pp​(X)3,Wp​(α,γ)≤Wp​(α,β)+Wp​(β,γ).
(46)
Proof

Symmetry is obtained by swapping the coordinates of a coupling. If the value is zero, an optimal coupling is supported on the diagonal and therefore the two marginals coincide. For the triangle inequality, glue optimal couplings π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β) and ξ∈Π(β,γ)\xi\in\Couplings(\be,\ga)ξ∈Π(β,γ) into σ\sigmaσ, project it to a coupling ρ\rhoρ between α\alα and γ\gaγ, and apply the ground triangle inequality plus Minkowski:

Wp(α,γ)≤(∫(d(x,y)+d(y,z))p dσ(x,y,z))1/p≤Wp(α,β)+Wp(β,γ).\Wass_p(\al,\ga) \leq \left(\int (d(x,y)+d(y,z))^p\d\sigma(x,y,z)\right)^{1/p} \leq \Wass_p(\al,\be)+\Wass_p(\be,\ga).Wp​(α,γ)≤(∫(d(x,y)+d(y,z))pdσ(x,y,z))1/p≤Wp​(α,β)+Wp​(β,γ).
(47)

Interpolation Induced By A Plan¶

The quadratic Wasserstein distance does not only compare two endpoint measures. An optimal plan also says how to move mass between them: each active pair (x,y)(x,y)(x,y) travels along the segment joining xxx to yyy. This turns an optimal coupling into a curve of measures.

Definition: W2\Wass_2W2​ Geodesic Induced By An Optimal Plan

Let α0,α1∈P2(Rd)\al_0,\al_1\in\Pp_2(\RR^d)α0​,α1​∈P2​(Rd), and let π⋆∈Π(α0,α1)\pi^\star\in\Couplings(\al_0,\al_1)π⋆∈Π(α0​,α1​) be optimal for W22(α0,α1)\Wass_2^2(\al_0,\al_1)W22​(α0​,α1​). For t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]t∈[0,1], define

et(x,y):=(1−t)x+ty,αt:=(et)♯π⋆.e_t(x,y)\eqdef(1-t)x+t y, \qquad \al_t\eqdef(e_t)_\sharp\pi^\star.et​(x,y):=(1−t)x+ty,αt​:=(et​)♯​π⋆.
(48)

The curve (αt)t∈[0,1](\al_t)_{t\in[0,1]}(αt​)t∈[0,1]​ is the displacement, or McCann, W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesic induced by π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆.

In the discrete case, each mass PijP_{ij}Pij​ moves from xix_ixi​ to yjy_jyj​ along its own segment. When the optimal plan is not induced by a map, one source atom can split into several moving atoms. If the optimal plan is not unique, different optimal plans may also induce different W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesics.

Algorithm: Displacement interpolation from a transport plan

Input: Measures α,β\alpha,\betaα,β on Rd\RR^dRd, time t∈[0,1]t\in[0,1]t∈[0,1].

Output: Displacement interpolant αt\alpha_tαt​.

Let π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆ be any minimizer of the quadratic Kantorovich problem.

Set interpolation map: et(x,y)=(1−t)x+ty.e_t(x,y)=(1-t)x+t y.et​(x,y)=(1−t)x+ty.

Push forward: αt=(et)♯π⋆.\al_t=(e_t)_\sharp\pi^\star.αt​=(et​)♯​π⋆.

If π⋆=∑i,jPij⋆δ(xi,yj)\pi^\star=\sum_{i,j}P^\star_{ij}\delta_{(x_i,y_j)}π⋆=∑i,j​Pij⋆​δ(xi​,yj​)​ then:

Compute αt=∑i,jPij⋆δ(1−t)xi+tyj.\al_t= \sum_{i,j}P^\star_{ij} \delta_{(1-t)x_i+t y_j}.αt​=∑i,j​Pij⋆​δ(1−t)xi​+tyj​​.

Return αt\alpha_tαt​.

Proposition: Optimal-Plan Interpolation Is A W2\Wass_2W2​ Geodesic

Let (αt)t∈[0,1](\al_t)_{t\in[0,1]}(αt​)t∈[0,1]​ be defined by Definition Interpolation Induced By A Plan. Then, for every 0≤s≤t≤10\leq s\leq t\leq10≤s≤t≤1,

W2(αs,αt)=(t−s)W2(α0,α1).\Wass_2(\al_s,\al_t) = (t-s)\Wass_2(\al_0,\al_1).W2​(αs​,αt​)=(t−s)W2​(α0​,α1​).
(49)

Thus t↦αtt\mapsto\al_tt↦αt​ is a constant-speed geodesic for the metric W2\Wass_2W2​.

Proof

Push the optimal plan π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆ forward by (es,et)(e_s,e_t)(es​,et​). This gives a coupling γs,t∈Π(αs,αt)\gamma_{s,t}\in\Couplings(\al_s,\al_t)γs,t​∈Π(αs​,αt​), and

∫∥z−z′∥2 dγs,t(z,z′)=∫∥et(x,y)−es(x,y)∥2 dπ⋆(x,y)=(t−s)2W22(α0,α1).\int \norm{z-z'}^2\d\gamma_{s,t}(z,z') = \int \norm{e_t(x,y)-e_s(x,y)}^2\d\pi^\star(x,y) = (t-s)^2\Wass_2^2(\al_0,\al_1).∫∥z−z′∥2dγs,t​(z,z′)=∫∥et​(x,y)−es​(x,y)∥2dπ⋆(x,y)=(t−s)2W22​(α0​,α1​).
(50)

Hence W2(αs,αt)≤(t−s)W2(α0,α1)\Wass_2(\al_s,\al_t)\leq(t-s)\Wass_2(\al_0,\al_1)W2​(αs​,αt​)≤(t−s)W2​(α0​,α1​). Applying this upper bound to the three pairs (0,s)(0,s)(0,s), (s,t)(s,t)(s,t) and (t,1)(t,1)(t,1), and using the triangle inequality of Proposition Proposition: Metric Property Of The Wasserstein Distance, gives

W2(α0,α1)≤W2(α0,αs)+W2(αs,αt)+W2(αt,α1)≤W2(α0,α1).\Wass_2(\al_0,\al_1) \leq \Wass_2(\al_0,\al_s)+\Wass_2(\al_s,\al_t)+\Wass_2(\al_t,\al_1) \leq \Wass_2(\al_0,\al_1).W2​(α0​,α1​)≤W2​(α0​,αs​)+W2​(αs​,αt​)+W2​(αt​,α1​)≤W2​(α0​,α1​).
(51)

All inequalities are therefore equalities, in particular the middle segment has the claimed length.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-plan-interpolation")

McCann interpolation induced by a non-deterministic optimal transport plan. In every panel, the red and blue endpoint measures are shown with low opacity, thin gray segments display the support Pij>tolP_{ij}>\mathrm{tol}Pij​>tol of the coupling, and the moving atoms are colored from red to blue along the interpolation.

The companion panel lets the same coupling be inspected along time ttt, with an entropy slider to contrast sparse and diffuse plans.

Interactive panel. Use the interpolation time and plan controls to see how a fixed coupling induces a cloud of displacement paths between endpoint measures.

General Geodesic Spaces¶

For Dirac masses in Euclidean space, the W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesic from δx\delta_xδx​ to δy\delta_yδy​ is t↦δ(1−t)x+tyt\mapsto\delta_{(1-t)x+t y}t↦δ(1−t)x+ty​. The same idea extends to any geodesic metric space (X,d)(\X,d)(X,d), meaning that each pair of points can be joined by a constant-speed metric geodesic. For each pair (x,y)(x,y)(x,y), one replaces the Euclidean segment by a curve γx,y:[0,1]→X\gamma^{x,y}:[0,1]\to\Xγx,y:[0,1]→X such that γ0x,y=x\gamma^{x,y}_0=xγ0x,y​=x, γ1x,y=y\gamma^{x,y}_1=yγ1x,y​=y, and

d(γsx,y,γtx,y)=∣t−s∣d(x,y).d(\gamma^{x,y}_s,\gamma^{x,y}_t)=|t-s|d(x,y).d(γsx,y​,γtx,y​)=∣t−s∣d(x,y).
(52)

If this geodesic is unique and depends measurably on (x,y)(x,y)(x,y), one defines et(x,y)=γtx,ye_t(x,y)=\gamma^{x,y}_tet​(x,y)=γtx,y​ and sets αt=(et)♯π⋆\al_t=(e_t)_\sharp\pi^\starαt​=(et​)♯​π⋆ for an optimal coupling π⋆\pi^\starπ⋆. When geodesics are not unique, there is no canonical interpolation of a pair of Diracs unless a choice is made: one may select a particular geodesic between xxx and yyy, or randomize among several such geodesics. The intrinsic formulation is to choose a probability measure η\etaη on the path space of constant-speed geodesics, called a dynamical optimal plan, such that (e0,e1)♯η(e_0,e_1)_\sharp\eta(e0​,e1​)♯​η is an optimal coupling, and to set αt=(et)♯η\al_t=(e_t)_\sharp\etaαt​=(et​)♯​η. Different measurable choices, or different conditional distributions over geodesics with the same endpoints, can give different W2\Wass_2W2​ geodesics; the constant-speed identity remains the same. This path-space viewpoint is standard in the general theory of Wasserstein spaces Ambrosio et al., 2006Villani, 2009Santambrogio, 2015.

Comparison With Monge¶

The distance Wp\Wass_pWp​ defined through the Kantorovich problem (45) should be contrasted with the directed distance W~\widetilde{\Wass}W obtained using Monge’s problem. The Kantorovich feasible set is never empty, since it contains the product coupling, although the ppp-cost may still be infinite without moment assumptions on non-compact spaces. By contrast, Monge’s constraint set {T:T♯α=β}\{T:T_\sharp\al=\be\}{T:T♯​α=β} can be empty. When an optimal Monge map exists, Kantorovich gives the same value by choosing the graph coupling (Id,T)♯α(\Id,T)_\sharp\alpha(Id,T)♯​α.

The next proposition makes precise one important sense in which Kantorovich is the relaxation of Monge. The cleanest statement is first made in the lifted plan variable π\piπ: deterministic graph couplings are dense among all couplings when the source can be split at arbitrarily fine scales. Thus the Kantorovich functional is the weak lower-semicontinuous envelope of the Monge graph functional.

Proposition: Kantorovich As The Plan-Space Relaxation Of Monge

Let (X,d)(\Xx,d)(X,d) be a compact metric space, let p≥1p\geq1p≥1, and let α,β∈P(X)\al,\be\in\Pp(\Xx)α,β∈P(X) with α\alα atomless. Define

G(α,β):={(Id,T)♯α:T♯α=β}⊂Π(α,β),\mathcal G(\al,\be) \eqdef \{(\Id,T)_\sharp\al:T_\sharp\al=\be\} \subset \Couplings(\al,\be),G(α,β):={(Id,T)♯​α:T♯​α=β}⊂Π(α,β),
(53)

and set Fp(π):=∫X×Xd(x,y)p dπ(x,y)F_p(\pi)\eqdef\int_{\Xx\times\Xx}d(x,y)^p\d\pi(x,y)Fp​(π):=∫X×X​d(x,y)pdπ(x,y). For every π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β), there are measurable maps TkT_kTk​ such that (Tk)♯α=β(T_k)_\sharp\al=\be(Tk​)♯​α=β, (Id,Tk)♯α⇀π(\Id,T_k)_\sharp\al\rightharpoonup\pi(Id,Tk​)♯​α⇀π, and Fp((Id,Tk)♯α)→Fp(π)F_p((\Id,T_k)_\sharp\al)\to F_p(\pi)Fp​((Id,Tk​)♯​α)→Fp​(π).

Consequently FpF_pFp​ is the weak lower-semicontinuous envelope on Π(α,β)\Couplings(\al,\be)Π(α,β) of the functional that equals FpF_pFp​ on graph couplings and +∞+\infty+∞ outside them. In particular,

Wp(α,β)p=inf⁡T♯α=β∫Xd(x,T(x))p dα(x)=W~p(α,β)p,\Wass_p(\al,\be)^p = \inf_{T_\sharp\al=\be}\int_\Xx d(x,T(x))^p\d\al(x) = \widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be)^p,Wp​(α,β)p=T♯​α=βinf​∫X​d(x,T(x))pdα(x)=Wp​(α,β)p,
(54)

as an equality of infimum values. The Monge infimum need not be attained.

Proof

Let π∈Π(α,β)\pi\in\Couplings(\al,\be)π∈Π(α,β). Choose finite Borel partitions (Ai)i(A_i)_i(Ai​)i​ and (Bj)j(B_j)_j(Bj​)j​ of X\XxX with mesh at most ϵ\epsilonϵ, and set mij=π(Ai×Bj)m_{ij}=\pi(A_i\times B_j)mij​=π(Ai​×Bj​). Since α\alα is atomless and ∑jmij=α(Ai)\sum_jm_{ij}=\al(A_i)∑j​mij​=α(Ai​), split each AiA_iAi​ into pieces AijA_{ij}Aij​ with α(Aij)=mij\al(A_{ij})=m_{ij}α(Aij​)=mij​. For mij>0m_{ij}>0mij​>0, Proposition Proposition: Existence Of Transport Maps From Atomless Sources gives a measurable map from AijA_{ij}Aij​ to BjB_jBj​ sending α∣Aij/mij\al|_{A_{ij}}/m_{ij}α∣Aij​​/mij​ to β∣Bj/β(Bj)\be|_{B_j}/\be(B_j)β∣Bj​​/β(Bj​). Pasting these maps gives T♯α=βT_\sharp\al=\beT♯​α=β, and the graph coupling (Id,T)♯α(\Id,T)_\sharp\al(Id,T)♯​α has the same masses as π\piπ on all rectangles Ai×BjA_i\times B_jAi​×Bj​.

Uniform continuity of every test function on the compact product implies that these graph couplings converge weakly to π\piπ as the mesh goes to zero. Applying this to the continuous cost dpd^pdp gives convergence of costs. Therefore any weakly lower-semicontinuous minorant of the graph functional is bounded above by FpF_pFp​ along the approximating graph couplings, while FpF_pFp​ itself is continuous and below the graph functional. This proves the envelope claim and then the equality of infima.

Since FpF_pFp​ is affine in the plan variable and Π(α,β)\Couplings(\al,\be)Π(α,β) is convex, this envelope is also the closed convex relaxation of the Monge graph problem in the space of transport plans.

At the level of endpoint measures, this gives a literal lower-semicontinuous-envelope interpretation for the Monge ppp-cost whenever source measures can be regularized into atomless ones.

Corollary: Lower-Semicontinuous Envelope Of The Monge p-Cost

Assume, in addition, that atomless probability measures are dense in P(X)\Pp(\Xx)P(X) for the Wp\Wass_pWp​ topology. Define

DM(α,β):=W~p(α,β)p=inf⁡T:X→XT♯α=β∫Xd(x,T(x))p dα(x),D_M(\al,\be) \eqdef \widetilde{\Wass}_p(\al,\be)^p = \inf_{\substack{T:\Xx\to\Xx\\T_\sharp\al=\be}} \int_\Xx d(x,T(x))^p\d\al(x),DM​(α,β):=Wp​(α,β)p=T:X→XT♯​α=β​inf​∫X​d(x,T(x))pdα(x),
(55)

with the convention DM(α,β)=+∞D_M(\al,\be)=+\inftyDM​(α,β)=+∞ if no admissible map exists. Then (α,β)↦Wp(α,β)p(\al,\be)\mapsto\Wass_p(\al,\be)^p(α,β)↦Wp​(α,β)p is the lower-semicontinuous envelope of DMD_MDM​ on P(X)×P(X)\Pp(\Xx)\times\Pp(\Xx)P(X)×P(X) for the product Wp\Wass_pWp​ topology.

Proof

For every admissible map TTT, the graph plan (Id,T)♯α(\Id,T)_\sharp\al(Id,T)♯​α is a coupling, hence Wp(α,β)p≤DM(α,β)\Wass_p(\al,\be)^p\leq D_M(\al,\be)Wp​(α,β)p≤DM​(α,β). Since Wpp\Wass_p^pWpp​ is continuous in the product Wp\Wass_pWp​ topology, it is a lower-semicontinuous minorant of DMD_MDM​.

Conversely, let HHH be any lower-semicontinuous minorant of DMD_MDM​. Fix (α,β)(\al,\be)(α,β) and choose atomless αk→α\al_k\to\alαk​→α in Wp\Wass_pWp​. By Proposition Proposition: Kantorovich As The Plan-Space Relaxation Of Monge, DM(αk,β)=Wp(αk,β)pD_M(\al_k,\be)=\Wass_p(\al_k,\be)^pDM​(αk​,β)=Wp​(αk​,β)p. Therefore H(α,β)≤lim inf⁡kH(αk,β)≤lim⁡kWp(αk,β)p=Wp(α,β)pH(\al,\be)\leq\liminf_k H(\al_k,\be)\leq \lim_k\Wass_p(\al_k,\be)^p=\Wass_p(\al,\be)^pH(α,β)≤liminfk​H(αk​,β)≤limk​Wp​(αk​,β)p=Wp​(α,β)p. Thus no larger lower-semicontinuous minorant exists.

The extra density assumption in the corollary is essential. If α\alα has atoms, the graph-density statement can fail dramatically: a single source Dirac mass cannot be mapped to two target Dirac masses. On finite spaces, the topology is discrete and this obstruction cannot be removed by closure. In such cases the Kantorovich formulation is not merely a closure of existing maps with the same marginals; it genuinely adds the possibility of splitting atomic mass.

Metric Properties: Topology And Applications¶

Wasserstein distances metrize weak convergence under moment control, sit between weak and strong topologies, and provide quantitative estimates in probability and robust optimization.

Proposition: Equivalence Of Wasserstein Distances On Compact Spaces

On a compact metric space, for p≤qp\leq qp≤q,

Wp(α,β)≤Wq(α,β)≤diam⁡(X)(q−p)/qWp(α,β)p/q.\Wass_p(\al,\be) \leq \Wass_q(\al,\be) \leq \diam(\Xx)^{(q-p)/q}\Wass_p(\al,\be)^{p/q}.Wp​(α,β)≤Wq​(α,β)≤diam(X)(q−p)/qWp​(α,β)p/q.
(56)
Proof

The left inequality is Jensen’s inequality applied to r↦rq/pr\mapsto r^{q/p}r↦rq/p. The right inequality follows from d(x,y)q≤diam⁡(X)q−pd(x,y)pd(x,y)^q\leq\diam(\Xx)^{q-p}d(x,y)^pd(x,y)q≤diam(X)q−pd(x,y)p.

Definition: Weak∗^*∗ Topology

A sequence (αk)k(\al_k)_k(αk​)k​ converges weakly∗^*∗ to α\alα in M+1(X)\Mm_+^1(\Xx)M+1​(X) if, for every bounded continuous function fff,

∫f dαk⟶∫f dα.\int f\d\al_k\longrightarrow\int f\d\al.∫fdαk​⟶∫fdα.
(57)
Remark: A Riemann-sum weak limit

On X=R\Xx=\RRX=R, the empirical measures on a regular grid satisfy

1n∑k=1nδk/n⇀U[0,1].\frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^n \de_{k/n} \rightharpoonup \Uu_{[0,1]}.n1​k=1∑n​δk/n​⇀U[0,1]​.
(58)

Indeed, for every continuous bounded function fff,

1n∑k=1nf(k/n)⟶∫01f(x) dx,\frac{1}{n} \sum_{k=1}^n f(k/n) \longrightarrow \int_0^1 f(x) \d x,n1​k=1∑n​f(k/n)⟶∫01​f(x)dx,
(59)

which is precisely the convergence of Riemann sums. This convergence is weak but not strong: for every nnn, the discrete measure and the uniform density are mutually singular, hence their total variation distance is equal to 2.

Remark: Weak convergence for discrete measures

In the special case of a single Dirac, δx(n)⇀δx\de_{x^{(n)}} \rightharpoonup \de_xδx(n)​⇀δx​ is equivalent to ∫f dδx(n)=f(x(n))→∫f dδx=f(x)\int f \d\de_{x^{(n)}} = f(x^{(n)}) \rightarrow \int f \d\de_{x} = f(x)∫fdδx(n)​=f(x(n))→∫fdδx​=f(x) for any continuous fff. This in turn is equivalent to x(n)→xx^{(n)} \rightarrow xx(n)→x. For a fixed number of atoms, if αn=∑i=1Nai(n)δxi(n)\al_n=\sum_{i=1}^N a_i^{(n)}\de_{x_i^{(n)}}αn​=∑i=1N​ai(n)​δxi(n)​​ and, after extracting a subsequence and relabeling, ai(n)→aia_i^{(n)}\to a_iai(n)​→ai​ and xi(n)→xix_i^{(n)}\to x_ixi(n)​→xi​, then αn\al_nαn​ converges weakly to ∑iaiδxi\sum_i a_i\de_{x_i}∑i​ai​δxi​​, with atoms at identical limits merged. Without a uniform bound on the number of atoms, weak limits of discrete measures can be non-discrete; empirical measures are the standard example.

Remark: Modes of convergence for random variables

Convergence of laws should be distinguished from stronger notions of convergence for random variables. If XnX_nXn​ and XXX are defined on a common probability space, then Xn→XX_n\to XXn​→X almost surely means pointwise convergence outside a null set, while convergence in probability means

∀ϵ>0,P(∥Xn−X∥>ϵ)→0.\foralls \epsilon>0,\qquad \PP(\norm{X_n-X}>\epsilon)\to0.∀ϵ>0,P(∥Xn​−X∥>ϵ)→0.
(60)

Almost-sure convergence implies convergence in probability, and convergence in probability implies convergence in law. Convergence in law is exactly weak∗^*∗ convergence of the probability measures (Xn)♯P⇀X♯P(X_n)_\sharp\PP\rightharpoonup X_\sharp\PP(Xn​)♯​P⇀X♯​P, and does not require all variables to live on the same probability space. Strong convergence of measures, for instance convergence in total variation, is different and usually much stronger: it controls the mass assigned to all measurable sets, not only averages against continuous test functions. In particular, total variation convergence implies weak convergence, but the converse fails for empirical approximations of continuous laws.

Remark: Central limit theorem

The central limit theorem states that if (Xi)i≥1(X_i)_{i\geq1}(Xi​)i≥1​ are i.i.d. random vectors with finite second moments, E(Xi)=0\EE(X_i)=0E(Xi​)=0, and E(XiXi⊤)=Id\EE(X_i X_i^\top)=\IdE(Xi​Xi⊤​)=Id, then the normalized sum

Zn:=1n∑i=1nXiZ_n \eqdef \frac{1}{\sqrt{n}} \sum_{i=1}^n X_iZn​:=n​1​i=1∑n​Xi​
(61)

converges in law toward the standard Gaussian N(0,Id)\Gaussian(0,\Id)N(0,Id). In the terminology recalled above, this means that the measure αn\al_nαn​ representing the law of ZnZ_nZn​ converges weakly, or weak∗^*∗ in the measure/test-function duality, toward the centered Gaussian measure α=N(0,Id)\al=\Gaussian(0,\Id)α=N(0,Id).

Equivalently, this is a statement about rescaled convolutions of measures. If μ\muμ and ν\nuν are probability measures on Rd\RR^dRd, their convolution is

μ∗ν:=add⁡♯(μ⊗ν),∫φ  d(μ∗ν)=∬φ(x+y)  dμ(x) dν(y)\mu*\nu \eqdef \operatorname{add}_\sharp(\mu\otimes\nu), \qquad \int \varphi\,\d(\mu*\nu) = \iint \varphi(x+y)\,\d\mu(x)\d\nu(y)μ∗ν:=add♯​(μ⊗ν),∫φd(μ∗ν)=∬φ(x+y)dμ(x)dν(y)
(62)

for every bounded continuous φ\varphiφ, where add⁡(x,y)=x+y\operatorname{add}(x,y)=x+yadd(x,y)=x+y. Thus μ∗ν\mu*\nuμ∗ν is the law of X+YX+YX+Y when XXX and YYY are independent with laws μ\muμ and ν\nuν. When μ\muμ and ν\nuν have densities fff and ggg, the convolution has density

(f∗g)(z)=∫Rdf(x)g(z−x) dx.(f*g)(z)=\int_{\RR^d} f(x)g(z-x)\d x.(f∗g)(z)=∫Rd​f(x)g(z−x)dx.
(63)

If μ\muμ is the common law of the variables XiX_iXi​, writing μ∗n\mu^{*n}μ∗n for the nnn-fold convolution of μ\muμ with itself, and denoting by Da(x)=axD_a(x)=a xDa​(x)=ax the dilation map, the law of ZnZ_nZn​ is

αn=(D1/n)♯μ∗n.\al_n=(D_{1/\sqrt n})_\sharp\mu^{*n}.αn​=(D1/n​​)♯​μ∗n.
(64)

The CLT therefore says that the normalized nnn-fold convolution (D1/n)♯μ∗n(D_{1/\sqrt n})_\sharp\mu^{*n}(D1/n​​)♯​μ∗n converges weak∗^*∗ to the Gaussian N(0,Id)\Gaussian(0,\Id)N(0,Id).

Proposition: Total Variation As Wasserstein For The Discrete Metric

Let ddd be the 0/10/10/1 metric, with d(x,x)=0d(x,x)=0d(x,x)=0 and d(x,y)=1d(x,y)=1d(x,y)=1 for x≠yx\neq yx=y. Then

Wp(α,β)p=12∥α−β∥TV.\Wass_p(\al,\be)^p = \frac12\|\al-\be\|_{\TV}.Wp​(α,β)p=21​∥α−β∥TV​.
(65)
Proof Sketch

For discrete measures on a common support, set ci=min⁡(ai,bi)c_i=\min(a_i,b_i)ci​=min(ai​,bi​). Any coupling has diagonal mass at most ∑ici\sum_i c_i∑i​ci​, so its off-diagonal cost is at least 1−∑ici1-\sum_i c_i1−∑i​ci​. This bound is achieved by putting cic_ici​ on the diagonal and coupling the leftover positive and negative parts by a product plan. Since

1−∑ici=12∑i∣ai−bi∣,1-\sum_i c_i = \frac12\sum_i |a_i-b_i|,1−i∑​ci​=21​i∑​∣ai​−bi​∣,
(66)

the formula follows.

For Dirac masses,

∥δxn−δx∥TV=2,Wp(δxn,δx)=d(xn,x).\|\delta_{x_n}-\delta_x\|_{\TV}=2, \qquad \Wass_p(\delta_{x_n},\delta_x)=d(x_n,x).∥δxn​​−δx​∥TV​=2,Wp​(δxn​​,δx​)=d(xn​,x).
(67)

Thus the strong topology never sees Diracs converge unless they are eventually equal, while the Wasserstein topology captures their spatial convergence.

Proposition: Wasserstein Metrizes Weak Convergence On Compact Spaces

If X\XxX is compact, then αk⇀α\al_k\rightharpoonup\alαk​⇀α if and only if Wp(αk,α)→0\Wass_p(\al_k,\al)\to0Wp​(αk​,α)→0.

Proof Sketch

For p=1p=1p=1, this is the Kantorovich--Rubinstein metrization theorem: by duality, W1\Wass_1W1​ is the supremum over 1-Lipschitz test functions, and on a compact metric space this class is compact modulo constants by Arzela--Ascoli. The comparison between Wp\Wass_pWp​ distances on compact spaces then gives the result for all p≥1p\geq1p≥1.

On non-compact spaces, one must also impose convergence of ppp-th moments: Wp(αk,α)→0\Wass_p(\alpha_k,\alpha)\to0Wp​(αk​,α)→0 if and only if αk⇀α\alpha_k\rightharpoonup\alphaαk​⇀α and

∫d(x,x0)p dαk(x)→∫d(x,x0)p dα(x).\int d(x,x_0)^p\d\alpha_k(x) \to \int d(x,x_0)^p\d\alpha(x).∫d(x,x0​)pdαk​(x)→∫d(x,x0​)pdα(x).
(68)

On a discrete space, weak and strong topologies coincide, and

dmin⁡2∥α−β∥TV≤W1(α,β)≤dmax⁡2∥α−β∥TV.\frac{d_{\min}}{2}\|\al-\be\|_{\TV} \leq \Wass_1(\al,\be) \leq \frac{d_{\max}}{2}\|\al-\be\|_{\TV}.2dmin​​∥α−β∥TV​≤W1​(α,β)≤2dmax​​∥α−β∥TV​.
(69)

Wasserstein Over Wasserstein¶

The construction can be iterated. Once (X,d)(\X,d)(X,d) is a metric space, the set of probability measures on X\XX becomes a metric space through Wp\Wass_pWp​. It can therefore serve as a new ground space. This is useful whenever the objects to compare are themselves random probability measures, or mixtures whose components are meaningful objects rather than only a collapsed density.

Proposition: Wasserstein Spaces As Ground Spaces

If (X,d)(\X,d)(X,d) is a Polish metric space, then Pp(X)\Pp_p(\X)Pp​(X) endowed with Wp\Wass_pWp​ is Polish. If X\XX is compact, then P(X)\Pp(\X)P(X) is compact for the Wasserstein topology, and the construction can be iterated to form P(P(X))\Pp(\Pp(\X))P(P(X)), P(P(P(X)))\Pp(\Pp(\Pp(\X)))P(P(P(X))), and so on.

Proof Sketch

Completeness follows by representing a Wp\Wass_pWp​-Cauchy sequence through almost optimally glued couplings, which gives a Cauchy random sequence whose law is the desired limit. Separability follows by approximating measures with finitely supported measures on a countable dense subset and rational weights. If X\XX is compact, Prokhorov compactness and Wasserstein metrization of weak convergence give compactness.

Elements of P2(P2(X))\Pp_2(\Pp_2(\X))P2​(P2​(X)) are probability laws over probability measures, or random probability measures. A parametric example is

A=(ζ↦αζ)♯γ.\mathfrak A=(\zeta\mapsto\alpha_\zeta)_\sharp\gamma.A=(ζ↦αζ​)♯​γ.
(70)
Definition: Collapsed, Or Barycentric, Mixture

The collapsed mixture associated with A\mathfrak AA is

∫Xf(x) dαˉA(x)=∫P2(X)(∫Xf(x) dα(x)) dA(α).\int_\X f(x)\d\bar\alpha_{\mathfrak A}(x) = \int_{\Pp_2(\X)} \left(\int_\X f(x)\d\alpha(x)\right) \d\mathfrak A(\alpha).∫X​f(x)dαˉA​(x)=∫P2​(X)​(∫X​f(x)dα(x))dA(α).
(71)

The Wasserstein distance on the Wasserstein space is

W22(A,B):=inf⁡Π∈Π(A,B)∫P2(X)×P2(X)W22(α,β) dΠ(α,β).\mathbb W_2^2(\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B) \eqdef \inf_{\Pi\in\Couplings(\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B)} \int_{\Pp_2(\X)\times\Pp_2(\X)} \Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta)\d\Pi(\alpha,\beta).W22​(A,B):=Π∈Π(A,B)inf​∫P2​(X)×P2​(X)​W22​(α,β)dΠ(α,β).
(72)

For Gaussian mixtures, this separates two geometries. A mixture can be viewed as a collapsed density on X\XX, or as a component law over Gaussian atoms in the Bures--Wasserstein space. For two component laws

A=∑iaiδN(mi,Σi),B=∑jbjδN(nj,Λj),\mathfrak A=\sum_i a_i\delta_{\Gaussian(m_i,\Sigma_i)}, \qquad \mathfrak B=\sum_j b_j\delta_{\Gaussian(n_j,\Lambda_j)},A=i∑​ai​δN(mi​,Σi​)​,B=j∑​bj​δN(nj​,Λj​)​,
(73)

the component-level problem uses the cost

Cij=∥mi−nj∥2+B(Σi,Λj)2.C_{ij}=\norm{m_i-n_j}^2+\Bb(\Sigma_i,\Lambda_j)^2.Cij​=∥mi​−nj​∥2+B(Σi​,Λj​)2.
(74)

If Π⋆\Pi^\starΠ⋆ is an optimal coupling between the weights aaa and bbb, and if AijA_{ij}Aij​ is the Brenier linear part from Σi\Sigma_iΣi​ to Λj\Lambda_jΛj​, each active pair follows the Gaussian geodesic

mij,t=(1−t)mi+tnj,Σij,t=((1−t)Id+tAij)Σi((1−t)Id+tAij).m_{ij,t}=(1-t)m_i+t n_j, \qquad \Sigma_{ij,t} = \big((1-t)\Id+tA_{ij}\big)\Sigma_i \big((1-t)\Id+tA_{ij}\big).mij,t​=(1−t)mi​+tnj​,Σij,t​=((1−t)Id+tAij​)Σi​((1−t)Id+tAij​).
(75)

Collapsing these component geodesics gives

αˉt=∑i,jΠij⋆N(mij,t,Σij,t).\bar\alpha_t= \sum_{i,j}\Pi^\star_{ij}\Gaussian(m_{ij,t},\Sigma_{ij,t}).αˉt​=i,j∑​Πij⋆​N(mij,t​,Σij,t​).
(76)

This component-level interpolation generally differs from the true W2\Wass_2W2​ interpolation between the collapsed mixture densities.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-wow-mixtures")

Two interpolations between the same asymmetric three-component one-dimensional Gaussian mixtures. The red endpoint has a broad central component carrying most of the mass, while the blue endpoint has two dominant sharp side modes. Left: Gaussian components are transported as atoms using their Bures--Wasserstein distance. Right: the collapsed densities are interpolated by the true one-dimensional quantile formula for W2\Wass_2W2​. The central mass is split and recombined in the collapsed geometry, making the two paths visibly distinct.

The interactive comparison keeps both geometries side by side: component-level transport moves Gaussian atoms, while collapsed transport rearranges the full density.

Interactive panel. Use the mixture and blur controls to compare transport between ordinary measures with transport between distributions of measures.

Proposition: Collapsing Is Non-Expansive

Let A,B∈P2(P2(X))\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B\in\Pp_2(\Pp_2(\X))A,B∈P2​(P2​(X)), and let αˉA\bar\alpha_{\mathfrak A}αˉA​ and βˉB\bar\beta_{\mathfrak B}βˉ​B​ be the collapsed mixtures. Then

W2(αˉA,βˉB)≤W2(A,B).\Wass_2(\bar\alpha_{\mathfrak A},\bar\beta_{\mathfrak B}) \leq \mathbb W_2(\mathfrak A,\mathfrak B).W2​(αˉA​,βˉ​B​)≤W2​(A,B).
(77)
Proof

Fix a coupling Π\PiΠ between A\mathfrak AA and B\mathfrak BB. For each (α,β)(\alpha,\beta)(α,β) choose an almost optimal coupling πα,β∈Π(α,β)\pi_{\alpha,\beta}\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)πα,β​∈Π(α,β). Integrating this Markov kernel against Π\PiΠ gives a coupling between the collapsed mixtures whose cost is bounded by the Π\PiΠ-average of W22(α,β)\Wass_2^2(\alpha,\beta)W22​(α,β). Taking infima proves the claim.

This viewpoint also clarifies lower bounds for Gromov--Wasserstein distances: a metric-measure space can be mapped to a law of local distance profiles, and these laws can be compared by Wasserstein-over-Wasserstein.

Remark: Local profiles as Wasserstein-over-Wasserstein laws

Given a metric-measure space X=(X,dX,μX)\XX=(\X,\dist_\X,\mu_\X)X=(X,dX​,μX​), each point defines a local distance distribution

αx=(dX(x,⋅))♯μX∈P(R+),DX=(x↦αx)♯μX∈P(P(R+)).\alpha_x=(\dist_\X(x,\cdot))_\sharp\mu_\X\in\Pp(\RR_+), \qquad \mathfrak D_\X=(x\mapsto\alpha_x)_\sharp\mu_\X\in\Pp(\Pp(\RR_+)).αx​=(dX​(x,⋅))♯​μX​∈P(R+​),DX​=(x↦αx​)♯​μX​∈P(P(R+​)).
(78)

The Memoli profile lower bound in Proposition Proposition: Memoli Profile Lower Bound is precisely a Wasserstein-over-Wasserstein comparison of these laws of local profiles. It replaces the full pairwise distortion by an ordinary OT problem whose ground cost is itself a one-dimensional Wasserstein distance.

Note that there exist alternative distances which also metricize weak convergence. The simplest ones are Hilbertian kernel norms, which are detailed in Section Dual Norms and Integral Probability Metrics.

Distributional Robustness And Wasserstein Infinity¶

Wasserstein distances define ambiguity sets around empirical laws. Given samples ziz_izi​ and α^n=1n∑iδzi\hat\alpha_n=\frac1n\sum_i\delta_{z_i}α^n​=n1​∑i​δzi​​, a distributionally robust optimization problem replaces empirical risk by

sup⁡β:Wp(β,α^n)≤ρ∫ℓθ(z) dβ(z).\sup_{\beta:\Wass_p(\beta,\hat\alpha_n)\leq\rho} \int \ell_\theta(z)\d\beta(z).β:Wp​(β,α^n​)≤ρsup​∫ℓθ​(z)dβ(z).
(79)

Under standard upper-semicontinuity and growth assumptions on the loss, one has the dual reformulation

sup⁡β:Wp(β,α^n)p≤ρp∫ℓθ dβ=inf⁡λ≥0λρp+1n∑i=1nsup⁡z{ℓθ(z)−λd(z,zi)p}.\sup_{\beta:\Wass_p(\beta,\hat\alpha_n)^p\leq\rho^p} \int \ell_\theta\d\beta = \inf_{\lambda\geq0} \lambda\rho^p + \frac1n\sum_{i=1}^n \sup_z\{\ell_\theta(z)-\lambda d(z,z_i)^p\}.β:Wp​(β,α^n​)p≤ρpsup​∫ℓθ​dβ=λ≥0inf​λρp+n1​i=1∑n​zsup​{ℓθ​(z)−λd(z,zi​)p}.
(80)

The robust risk is therefore an empirical risk in which each sample is replaced by its worst penalized perturbation. For p=1p=1p=1 and an LθL_\thetaLθ​-Lipschitz loss,

sup⁡β:W1(β,α^n)≤ρ∫ℓθ dβ≤1n∑iℓθ(zi)+ρLθ.\sup_{\beta:\Wass_1(\beta,\hat\alpha_n)\leq\rho} \int \ell_\theta\d\beta \leq \frac1n\sum_i\ell_\theta(z_i)+\rho L_\theta.β:W1​(β,α^n​)≤ρsup​∫ℓθ​dβ≤n1​i∑​ℓθ​(zi​)+ρLθ​.
(81)

Figure Notebook-code shows this robustification as a spatial perturbation picture. The Wasserstein ball permits empirical atoms to move in the data geometry, while the global transport budget controls how many atoms can move far toward high-loss regions.

show_book_figure("kantorovich-dro-ambiguity")

Wasserstein distributional robustness as a geometric ambiguity set. The red empirical atoms sit next to a dashed linear decision boundary. Local disks suggest admissible spatial perturbations, and the last panel shows a simple budgeted adversary moving atoms along a fixed loss-gradient direction.

Proposition: Convexity Of Transport Costs

For any nonnegative lower-semicontinuous cost ccc, the value

(α,β)↦Lc(α,β)(\alpha,\beta)\mapsto\mathcal{L}_c(\alpha,\beta)(α,β)↦Lc​(α,β)
(82)

is jointly convex. In particular, (α,β)↦Wp(α,β)p(\alpha,\beta)\mapsto\Wass_p(\alpha,\beta)^p(α,β)↦Wp​(α,β)p is jointly convex. The distance W1\Wass_1W1​ is jointly convex, but Wp\Wass_pWp​ itself need not be convex for p>1p>1p>1.

Proof

Let π0\pi_0π0​ and π1\pi_1π1​ be nearly optimal couplings for (α0,β0)(\alpha_0,\beta_0)(α0​,β0​) and (α1,β1)(\alpha_1,\beta_1)(α1​,β1​). Then (1−t)π0+tπ1(1-t)\pi_0+t\pi_1(1−t)π0​+tπ1​ is a coupling between the convex combinations of the marginals, and its cost is the corresponding convex combination. Taking infima proves joint convexity. For p>1p>1p>1, the root can destroy convexity; on the line, Wp((1−t)δ0+tδ1,δ0)=t1/p\Wass_p((1-t)\delta_0+t\delta_1,\delta_0)=t^{1/p}Wp​((1−t)δ0​+tδ1​,δ0​)=t1/p is concave.

The limiting distance

W∞(α,β):=inf⁡π∈Π(α,β)ess sup⁡(x,y)∼πd(x,y)\Wass_\infty(\alpha,\beta) \eqdef \inf_{\pi\in\Couplings(\alpha,\beta)} \esssup_{(x,y)\sim\pi} d(x,y)W∞​(α,β):=π∈Π(α,β)inf​(x,y)∼πesssup​d(x,y)
(83)

minimizes the worst displacement rather than an average displacement.

Proposition: W∞\Wass_\inftyW∞​ Robust Envelope Around An Empirical Law

Let (Z,d)(\Zz,d)(Z,d) be a Polish metric space. Let α^=∑i=1naiδzi\hat\alpha=\sum_{i=1}^n a_i\delta_{z_i}α^=∑i=1n​ai​δzi​​ with ai>0a_i>0ai​>0 and ∑iai=1\sum_i a_i=1∑i​ai​=1, and assume the closed balls B‾(zi,ρ)\overline B(z_i,\rho)B(zi​,ρ) are compact. For any real-valued upper-semicontinuous loss ℓ\ellℓ,

sup⁡β:W∞(β,α^)≤ρ∫ℓ(z) dβ(z)=∑i=1naisup⁡z∈B‾(zi,ρ)ℓ(z).\sup_{\beta:\Wass_\infty(\beta,\hat\alpha)\leq\rho} \int \ell(z)\d\beta(z) = \sum_{i=1}^n a_i \sup_{z\in\overline B(z_i,\rho)}\ell(z).β:W∞​(β,α^)≤ρsup​∫ℓ(z)dβ(z)=i=1∑n​ai​z∈B(zi​,ρ)sup​ℓ(z).
(84)
Proof

Any feasible coupling between α^\hat\alphaα^ and β\betaβ disintegrates as ∑iaiδzi⊗νi\sum_i a_i\delta_{z_i}\otimes\nu_i∑i​ai​δzi​​⊗νi​, with each νi\nu_iνi​ supported in B‾(zi,ρ)\overline B(z_i,\rho)B(zi​,ρ). Hence the robust expectation is bounded above by the displayed sum. The reverse inequality follows by choosing, for each iii, a maximizer zi⋆∈B‾(zi,ρ)z_i^\star\in\overline B(z_i,\rho)zi⋆​∈B(zi​,ρ) and taking β=∑iaiδzi⋆\beta=\sum_i a_i\delta_{z_i^\star}β=∑i​ai​δzi⋆​​.

Theoretical Application: Quantitative Central Limit Theorems¶

Weak topology says whether laws converge; Wasserstein distances also quantify how fast. As recalled in Remark Remark: Central limit theorem, the central limit theorem first appears as a qualitative weak∗^*∗ convergence of the rescaled convolution laws toward a Gaussian. The next result is its quantitative counterpart: the same convergence becomes a rate estimate in W1\Wass_1W1​, which controls the error of all 1-Lipschitz observables of the normalized sum.

show_book_figure("matching-quantitative-clt")

Central-limit theorem for normalized Bernoulli sums. Starting from α0=12(δ−1+δ1)\alpha_0=\frac12(\delta_{-1}+\delta_1)α0​=21​(δ−1​+δ1​), the law of Zn=n−1/2∑iXiZ_n=n^{-1/2}\sum_i X_iZn​=n−1/2∑i​Xi​ remains discrete, but its rescaled atom heights approach the standard Gaussian density shown in gray.

The interactive demo varies the number of summands and the Bernoulli skew, making the Wasserstein view of weak convergence visible even while the law remains discrete.

Interactive panel. Use the dimension and sample-size controls to watch the Wasserstein CLT scaling predicted by Lipschitz test functions.

Proposition: Berry--Esseen Bound In W1\Wass_1W1​

Let (Xi)i=1n(X_i)_{i=1}^n(Xi​)i=1n​ be i.i.d. real random variables with EXi=0\mathbb{E}X_i=0EXi​=0, EXi2=1\mathbb{E}X_i^2=1EXi2​=1 and E∣Xi∣3<+∞\mathbb{E}|X_i|^3<+\inftyE∣Xi​∣3<+∞. If αn\alpha_nαn​ is the law of n−1/2∑iXin^{-1/2}\sum_i X_in−1/2∑i​Xi​ and γ\gammaγ is the standard Gaussian law, then

W1(αn,γ)≤C E∣X1∣3n,\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\gamma) \leq \frac{C\,\mathbb{E}|X_1|^3}{\sqrt n},W1​(αn​,γ)≤n​CE∣X1​∣3​,
(85)

where CCC is a universal constant.

Proof Sketch

By Kantorovich--Rubinstein duality,

W1(αn,γ)=sup⁡Lip⁡(h)≤1∣Eh(Sn)−Eh(G)∣.\Wass_1(\alpha_n,\gamma) = \sup_{\Lip(h)\leq1} \left|\mathbb{E}h(S_n)-\mathbb{E}h(G)\right|.W1​(αn​,γ)=Lip(h)≤1sup​∣Eh(Sn​)−Eh(G)∣.
(86)

For each such hhh, solve Stein’s equation fh′(x)−xfh(x)=h(x)−Eh(G)f_h'(x)-xf_h(x)=h(x)-\mathbb{E}h(G)fh′​(x)−xfh​(x)=h(x)−Eh(G). The solution has uniform derivative bounds depending only on the Lipschitz constant of hhh. Expanding by replacing summands one at a time, the first- and second-order terms cancel because EXi=0\mathbb{E}X_i=0EXi​=0 and EXi2=1\mathbb{E}X_i^2=1EXi2​=1. The Taylor remainder is bounded by C∑iE∣Xi/n∣3C\sum_i\mathbb{E}|X_i/\sqrt n|^3C∑i​E∣Xi​/n​∣3, giving the n−1/2n^{-1/2}n−1/2 rate Berry, 1941Esseen, 1942Chen et al., 2011Bobkov, 2018Rio, 2011.

References¶
  1. Kantorovich, L. (1942). On the transfer of masses (in Russian). Doklady Akademii Nauk, 37(2), 227–229.
  2. Villani, C. (2003). Topics in Optimal Transportation (Vol. 58). American Mathematical Society.
  3. Villani, C. (2009). Optimal Transport: Old and New (Vol. 338). Springer.
  4. Rachev, S. T., & Rüschendorf, L. (1998). Mass Transportation Problems: Volume II: Applications. Springer.
  5. Dantzig, G. B. (1951). Application of the simplex method to a transportation problem. Activity Analysis of Production and Allocation, 13, 359–373.
  6. Bertsekas, D. P., & Eckstein, J. (1988). Dual coordinate step methods for linear network flow problems. Mathematical Programming, 42(1), 203–243.
  7. Orlin, J. B. (1997). A polynomial time primal network simplex algorithm for minimum cost flows. Mathematical Programming, 78(2), 109–129.
  8. Ambrosio, L., Gigli, N., & Savaré, G. (2006). Gradient Flows in Metric Spaces and in the Space of Probability Measures. Springer.
  9. Santambrogio, F. (2015). Optimal Transport for Applied Mathematicians: Calculus of Variations, PDEs, and Modeling. Birkhäuser.
  10. Berry, A. C. (1941). The Accuracy of the Gaussian Approximation to the Sum of Independent Variates. Transactions of the American Mathematical Society, 49(1), 122–136.
  11. Esseen, C.-G. (1942). On the Liapunoff Limit of Error in the Theory of Probability. Arkiv for Matematik, Astronomi Och Fysik, 28A(9), 1–19.
  12. Chen, L. H. Y., Goldstein, L., & Shao, Q.-M. (2011). Normal Approximation by Stein’s Method. Springer.
  13. Bobkov, S. G. (2018). Berry–Esseen bounds and Edgeworth expansions in the central limit theorem for transport distances. Probability Theory and Related Fields, 170, 229–262.
  14. Rio, E. (2011). Asymptotic constants for minimal distance in the central limit theorem. Electronic Communications in Probability, 16, 96–103.
